text: layers · parallel depth · all present simultaneously which depth? a layered structure is a stack of parallel planes, all present at the same time at the same location. the skin and the transversalis fascia exist together, separated by depth, not by route. a defect at one depth shows up as a symptom at the surface — but the symptom does not tell you the depth. the job is to localize how deep . layer 1 — surface layer 2 layer 3 layer 4 — defect lives here layer 5 layer 6 — source of truth symptom × defect depth → defect at depth · symptom at the surface · localize by going deeper anatomical examples abdominal wall (skin → fascia → muscle → transversalis → peritoneum). skin and its appendages. meninges (dura → arachnoid → pia). cornea. bowel wall histologic layers. any structure where the diagnostic question is " how deep is the lesion ." software parallel the rendering stack. a request passes through every layer at once on its way out — html → dom bindings → state store → cache → network → server. a bug at one layer manifests as wrong pixels at the surface. you localize by inspecting each depth in turn. diagnostic logic start at the symptom. move one layer deeper. ask: is the data correct at this depth? if yes, the lesion is shallower than you thought; if no, keep going. you stop at the first boundary where normal becomes abnormal — that is the lesion. treatment happens at that depth, not at the surface where the symptom appeared. failure signature a layer defect produces referred symptoms at the surface . indirect hernia (defect at transversalis fascia) bulges through the skin. the patient points to skin; you fix the fascia.

label: 2 — Conduits
text: 2 — conduits · 2

label: Conduits
text: conduits · serial stations · flow moves through sequentially which station? a conduit is a series of functional stations connected by directional flow. production at one end, expulsion at the other. every station does a job. when the flow stalls or the cargo is contaminated, the symptom appears at the exit — but the lesion is at the station where flow first failed. the job is to localize which station , by tracing upstream from where the flow visibly breaks. 1 production 2 collection 3 transport × lesion 5 control 6 expulsion ↑ pressure / upstream damage "no output" flow direction flow stops at station 4 · pressure backs up to stations upstream · symptom shows at the exit anatomical examples the urinary tract (production → collection → transport → storage → control → expulsion). the biliary tree. the gi tract. the airway. csf flow. anywhere with a defined source, sequential processing, and an exit. software parallel data processing pipelines, ci/cd, upload → transform → store. a failed deploy at the "test" stage halts everything downstream; everything upstream queues. you localize by walking the pipeline view from the failed stage upward — same move as tracing hydronephrosis up the ureter to the stone. diagnostic logic find where the flow visibly stops. trace upstream. every station above the blockage shows back-pressure damage (dilation, distention, parenchymal injury). every station below shows under-perfusion (atrophy, no output, downstream dependents starved). the lesion is at the station where flow first fails — not at the symptomatic exit and not at the dilated stations above. failure signature a conduit lesion produces upstream back-pressure and downstream absence . renal stone at the uvj (station 3): hydronephrosis above (stations 1-2 distended), empty ureter below, patient pointing at the flank where the dilated kidney capsule hurts.

label: 3 — Networks
text: 3 — networks · 3

label: Networks
text: networks · branching · distributed · multiple redundant paths which territory? a network is a tree of branches descending from one or more sources, fanning out to a wide territory. a signal or supply travels along multiple paths simultaneously, and a defect at any branch starves only the territory it served — leaving the rest of the network functional. the deficit is the map. the job is to read which branch was lost by reading what's missing downstream . affected territory s source × occluded one branch occluded · the deficit reveals the branch point · map the territory, find the lesion anatomical examples vasculature (arteries and veins). lymphatics. the peripheral and central nervous system. bronchial tree. hepatic portal system. anywhere a single source supplies a fan of dependent territories through branches. software parallel event systems, pub/sub, webhooks, auth propagation. an auth token touches middleware, route guards, websocket connections, multiple endpoints. a failure in token refresh doesn't stall at one station — it causes simultaneous failure across every downstream consumer. the diagnostic is "what's the territory served by this branch?" diagnostic logic read the deficit. the pattern of what is missing — which muscles are weak, which sensory dermatome is numb, which microservice stopped responding — defines the territory. the territory defines the branch. you localize by inversion: the network is intact except for one downstream area, so the lesion is at the last branch point upstream of that area. failure signature a network lesion produces distributed simultaneous failure of a single territory , sparing everything else. stroke of the mca: contralateral face and arm weakness, expressive aphasia if left-sided. the territory is the diagnosis.

label: The Diagnostic Question Table
text: the diagnostic question table · pinned, because this is the thing to memorize. every clinical encounter starts with naming the geometry of the structure involved — then the diagnostic question is determined. geometry the diagnostic question find the lesion by treatment principle layers "which depth?" going deeper until normal becomes abnormal. treat at the depth of the defect — not at the surface where the symptom appears. conduits "which station?" tracing upstream from where flow visibly stops. treat at the station where flow first failed — not at the upstream dilation or the silent exit. networks "which territory?" mapping what's missing; inferring the branch point upstream of it. treat the branch — sometimes by reopening it, sometimes by accepting the territory and supporting collaterals.

label: Combinations
text: combinations · pure geometries are rare. most organs are combinations. recognizing the mix is part of the skill. kidney — a conduit at the macro level (it sits at the head of the urinary tract), but internally a layered network: glomerulus → tubule → collecting duct (layers of filtration physiology) supplied by a branching vasculature. renal infarct is a network problem; nephrotic syndrome is a layer problem; obstruction at the upj is a conduit problem. one organ, three geometries, three diagnostic moves. skin — primarily layers (epidermis / dermis / subcutaneous), but vascularized by a branching network and drained by lymphatics. cellulitis is mostly layer; lymphangitis is a network read; necrotizing fasciitis is layers plus a network catastrophe inside them. lung — bronchial tree is a network of conduits ending in alveolar layers. pneumonia is a layer problem at the alveolus; pe is a network problem at the pulmonary artery; mucus plugging is a conduit problem in a bronchus. the interview question when you don't know what to do, ask: "what geometry am i looking at, and what's the corresponding diagnostic question?" if you can answer that, the next test usually picks itself. if you can't, you don't understand the anatomy well enough to act yet — and the right move is to stop and figure that out, not to keep ordering things.

label: How This Reference Is Organized
text: how this reference is organized · each subsection of this reference covers one geometry and one specialty as its exemplar. start with the foundational geometry doc, then read the specialty as a worked example of that pattern in clinical practice. geometry specialty exemplar what you'll learn there layers the abdominal wall (in progress) hernia anatomy and the layer-by-layer diagnostic approach. the original case study. conduits the urinary tract production → expulsion as a six-station pipeline. obstruction, infection, malignancy, neurogenic failure — all framed as station-level lesions. networks vascular trees (planned) stroke, mi, peripheral arterial disease, deep vein thrombosis. territory reading as the diagnostic skill. ← back to reference written may 24, 2026 · for pas, medical students, and clinical clerks

label: The Urinary Tract — A Conduit
text: the urinary tract — a conduit · five functional stations from production to expulsion. urine flows one way. symptoms appear at the exit. the lesion is upstream — and your job is to localize which station first failed.

label: The Frame
text: the frame · the urinary tract is a conduit — the second of the three geometries . that changes the diagnostic question. you are not asking "how deep is the lesion?" the way you do with the abdominal wall. you are asking "which station did the flow first fail at?" conduits fail in characteristic ways. block one and pressure builds upstream while the exit goes silent. contaminate one with bacteria and the infection rides the flow downstream and rises upstream. lose the wall integrity and the cargo leaks out where it shouldn't. the shape of the failure tells you which station. the diagnostic principle, once find where the flow visibly fails — that's the symptom layer. then trace upstream . every station above the failure shows back-pressure damage (dilation, distention, parenchymal injury). every station below shows under-perfusion (atrophy, silence, no output). the lesion sits at the boundary where back-pressure ends and silence begins. the conduit, in situ. kidneys → ureters → bladder → prostate-encircled urethra → exit. every clinical complaint downstream points to a station along this path. keep the picture in mind while reading the stations below. from burroughs wellcome co., atlas of the urinary tract .

label: The Five Stations
text: the five stations · numbered in the direction of flow. production at the top, expulsion at the bottom. every station has a job and a characteristic failure mode. 1 production kidney 2 transport ureter 3 storage bladder · detrusor 4 control sphincters · neural 5 expulsion urethra · outlet direction of flow · production → expulsion 5 stations · flow is one-way · trace upstream from the failure 1

label: Production · kidney
text: production · kidney · glomerulus filters plasma. tubules concentrate. collecting ducts deliver urine to the calyces and renal pelvis — the kidney's internal drainage.

label: Failure looks like:
text: failure looks like: · hematuria from glomerular or parenchymal source. pyelonephritis. renal masses. aki from prerenal, intrarenal, or postrenal causes. hydronephrosis when station 2 is blocked. 2

label: Transport · ureter
text: transport · ureter · 25 cm muscular tube. peristalsis moves urine against gravity. three natural narrowings: upj, pelvic brim, uvj — all common stone-arrest points.

label: Storage · bladder
text: storage · bladder · compliant reservoir. stores at low pressure (the critical mechanical property). trigone is sensory-rich. detrusor contracts on command to empty.

label: Control · sphincters & neural
text: control · sphincters & neural · two gates. internal sphincter (smooth muscle, sympathetic α1, involuntary) at the bladder neck. external sphincter (striated muscle, pudendal nerve, voluntary) at the membranous urethra. pons coordinates.

label: Expulsion · urethra & outlet
text: expulsion · urethra & outlet · the exit conduit. in men, traverses the prostate — a layer-pattern structure wrapped around a conduit-pattern lumen. stricture, compression, and obstruction all live here.

label: Station 1 Has Its Own Geometry Inside
text: station 1 has its own geometry inside · the kidney sits at the head of the conduit, but internally it is a small layered network of its own — filtration physiology arranged as a flow path. worth zooming in once, because urology bugs at this level have a different feel from anything else in the tract. g glomerulus filtration t tubule concentration cd collecting duct final regulation c calyx gathering p pelvis → ureter internal flow within station 1 the kidney is itself a small pipeline · failures here are sub-station problems where the failure sits within the kidney changes the disease completely. a glomerular lesion is one disease (proteinuria, nephritic syndromes). a tubular lesion is another (concentrating defects, aki). a collecting-duct / calyx / pelvis lesion is a urological problem (stones in the calyx, upj obstruction at the pelvis-ureter junction). same organ, three sub-stations, three different specialties. where the geometries overlap the kidney is a conduit station from the urinary tract's point of view, but internally it is filtration physiology (layers — nephrons stacked) supplied by a vascular tree (a network). that is why a kidney can fail in three different ways: parenchymal disease (layer problem), vascular event (network problem — e.g. renal infarct), and collecting-system obstruction (conduit problem — e.g. stone). each has a different specialist, a different test, a different fix. station 1, in cross-section. cortex and medulla make urine; calyces gather it; the pelvis hands it off to the ureter. obstruction anywhere downstream backs up into this architecture — flattening calyces (hydronephrosis), thinning cortex (chronic damage), and ultimately silencing the nephron. from burroughs wellcome co., atlas of the urinary tract .

label: Physiology — A Two-Phase State Machine
text: physiology — a two-phase state machine · stations 3 and 4 work together as a state machine. the conduit is always in one of two states. the transitions are coordinated by the pontine micturition center. phase detrusor (st. 3) internal sphincter (st. 4) external sphincter (st. 4) storage sympathetic dominant (t10–l2) relaxed (β3) — bladder fills at low pressure closed (α1) tonic contraction (pudendal) voiding parasympathetic dominant (s2–s4) contracts (m3) opens reflexively voluntary relaxation three things have to be true for voiding to work: the detrusor has to generate pressure, both sphincters have to open, and the upstream stations have to have something to deliver. loss of coordination — detrusor contracting against a closed sphincter (dyssynergia) — generates high pressure with no flow. that hemodynamic insult is identical to bladder outlet obstruction and damages stations 1–2 the same way.

label: What maintains equilibrium
text: what maintains equilibrium · low storage pressure. the detrusor accommodates >400 ml with almost no rise in pressure. high pressure during storage is pathologic — it back-pressures station 2 and damages station 1. sphincter–detrusor coordination. when the detrusor contracts, the sphincters open. lose this and you have no-flow voiding at high pressure. sensation matches volume. first urge ~150 ml, strong urge ~300 ml, capacity ~400–500 ml. mismatch points to a sensory or afferent issue at station 3. the useful reframe most lower-tract symptoms are a failure of one of three things: storage (urgency, frequency, incontinence — station 3), emptying (hesitancy, weak stream, retention — station 4 or 5), or coordination (post-void dribble, high-pressure low-flow — station 4). pick the bucket first. the differential collapses immediately.

label: Pathophysiology as Station Failure
text: pathophysiology as station failure · four mechanisms account for almost everything. for each: which station holds the lesion, which station shows the symptom, and what's the back-pressure pattern that gives it away. mechanism lesion station symptom shows at upstream signature obstruction stone, bph, stricture, tumor, clot wherever the lumen narrows (2 and 5 most common) the exit goes silent (no output, weak stream) or the patient feels pain at the dilated upstream station dilation above the lesion — hydronephrosis if upper tract, distended bladder if outlet infection cystitis, pyelonephritis, prostatitis wherever bacteria colonize (1, 3, 5) dysuria at the exit (st. 5); systemic signs if station 1 is involved vital signs change with station depth. cystitis: afebrile. pyelonephritis: febrile, ill. same family of bacteria, two stations, two diseases. neurogenic dysfunction sci, ms, diabetes, stroke above the conduit — brain, cord, peripheral nerve stations 3 and 4 behave wrong without intrinsic disease imaging the bladder or sphincter is unrevealing. the lesion is in the wiring, not the conduit. malignancy rcc, urothelial ca, prostate ca any station; tissue-specific hematuria at the exit, or asymptomatic incidental finding often no upstream signature until the tumor obstructs. painless gross hematuria is malignancy until proven otherwise — precisely because there is no back-pressure to alert you. the trap "burning when i pee" is a station 5 symptom. the reflex is to treat for cystitis at station 3. but the same symptom comes from urethritis (station 5), prostatitis (station 5-adjacent), a distal stone wedged at the uvj (boundary of stations 2 and 3), or atrophic vaginitis next door. the urinalysis isn't a confirmation test — it's a localization test. a clean ua in a "uti" pushes you off station 3 immediately and forces you to look elsewhere.

label: Two pictures that earn their place
text: two pictures that earn their place · what follows are the two single most useful illustrations in this whole document. each shows the conduit with every conceivable lesion at every station, in one drawing. look at them long enough that you can localize a presentation to a station by pattern recognition — that's what the cards on call will demand of you. stations 1–2: what can go wrong upstream. stones in the calyx and along the ureter. ureteral tumor. kink. aberrant vessel compressing from outside. scar tissue. chronic infection. every one of them produces hydronephrosis above and a silent system below — the conduit's signature failure pattern. boxed labels indicate change due to obstruction. from burroughs wellcome co., atlas of the urinary tract . stations 3–5: what can go wrong downstream. the bladder has hypertrophied to push against an obstruction. the prostate is enlarged (bph) and carcinomatous. there's a diverticulum, a calculus, a congenital valve, a polyp, a stricture, and phimosis at the meatus. any one of these explains "can't pee" or "weak stream." the trainee's job is to identify which. from burroughs wellcome co., atlas of the urinary tract .

label: Diagnostic Algorithm — Matched to Station
text: diagnostic algorithm — matched to station · every test you have access to images a different segment of the conduit. you don't pick the test based on the symptom; you pick it based on which station you still need to see. know what each test reveals — and just as importantly, where you remain blind. test station(s) it images what it answers where you're blind urinalysis the cargo — not the conduit is there blood, infection, protein, glucose, crystals? cannot localize. blood in ua could be from any station. urine culture the cargo, with organism id what organism, what sensitivity? doesn't tell you which station is colonized. post-void residual station 3 function does the bladder empty? doesn't tell you why it didn't empty — obstruction vs. weak detrusor. uroflow + urodynamics stations 3, 4, 5 — functional storage pressure, voiding pressure, flow rate, sphincter coordination no anatomy. a flat-line flow says something is obstructed — not where. cystoscopy stations 3, 4, 5 — direct visualization of the lumen what does the inside of the conduit actually look like? stones, tumors, strictures, prostatic encroachment. only what's inside the lumen. a 4 cm renal mass at station 1 is invisible. ultrasound (renal + bladder) stations 1, 3 — structural hydronephrosis, bladder volume, stones, masses bowel gas obscures station 2 (ureter). operator-dependent. ct urogram stations 1, 2, 3 — full upper-tract structural stones, masses, hydronephrosis, anatomy function. a dilated system could be obstructed now or scarred from years ago. mri pelvis stations 3, 4, 5 — soft tissue detail prostate cancer staging, pelvic floor anatomy stones (not bright on mri). acute infection. biopsy tissue diagnosis at the lesion station what is this growth, histologically? sampling error. a negative biopsy rules out cancer in the cores you took — nothing more. the pattern tests get more invasive as you go deeper into the conduit's interior. ua is free and reads the cargo. cystoscopy is direct and invasive. biopsy is tissue and committed. you earn the right to a deep test by failing to localize with a shallow one. when a senior asks "what's the next test?" they are really asking "which station are you still blind to?" answer that and the test picks itself.

label: Treatment — Fix at the Station of the Lesion
text: treatment — fix at the station of the lesion · this is the discipline that separates conduit thinking from symptomatic management. the temptation is to treat where the patient is uncomfortable — at the exit. the discipline is to treat where the flow first failed.

label: BPH: the canonical example
text: bph: the canonical example · an enlarged prostate compresses the prostatic urethra (station 5). the patient presents with retention — a station 3 functional consequence and an exit complaint ("i can't go"). the bladder is innocent. it has been pumping against an outlet that won't open. pressure builds backward through station 2 to station 1, where the kidneys are paying the bill. putting a catheter in fixes the exit — urine comes out. but the prostate is still there. stop the catheter, the retention returns. the lesion is at station 5. treatment options all act there: α-blocker (tamsulosin) — relaxes smooth muscle at the bladder neck and prostatic urethra. acts on stations 4–5. 5α-reductase inhibitor (finasteride) — shrinks the prostate over months. acts on the structure itself. turp / holep / urolift — surgically remove or retract the obstructing tissue. definitive station 5 intervention. catheter management treats the symptom. each of the above treats the lesion. both are sometimes appropriate — but you should always know which one you are doing and why.

label: The principle generalizes
text: the principle generalizes · presentation where the patient feels it lesion station where you treat renal colic flank → groin (referred up the dermatome) stone at station 2 station 2 — pass, lithotripsy, ureteroscopy, stent painless gross hematuria color of urine anywhere from station 1 to 5 wherever the lesion is localized — never the exit unless it's there urgency / frequency "i have to go" detrusor overactivity (station 3) or sensory afferent (station 3) station 3 — antimuscarinic, β3 agonist, behavioral, bont stress incontinence leak with cough sphincter / pelvic floor (station 4) station 4 — pelvic floor pt, sling, bulking agent recurrent uti in a man dysuria anatomic abnormality enabling colonization — stone, residual, prostate the enabling lesion. antibiotics handle this episode; they don't fix the cause. the discipline "stop the bleeding, stop the pain, drain the obstruction" is emergency medicine. it buys time. it is not the operation. once the patient is stable, ask the question every time: which station first failed, and what treats it there? then plan the definitive treatment at that station.

label: A Worked Case — "I Can't Pee"
text: a worked case — "i can't pee" · 68-year-old man. presents to the ed. suprapubic fullness, hasn't urinated in 14 hours, last attempt produced "a few drops." walk it up the conduit. 1 the exit complaint · station 5 "i can't pee." flow has visibly stopped at the exit. you do not yet know what station first failed. suprapubic fullness on exam — bladder is palpable, dull to percussion above the symphysis. the symptom is at station 5. the lesion could be anywhere from 3 upward — the bladder isn't emptying, but you don't know why yet. decision: bladder scan before anything else. 2 locate the backed-up cargo · station 3 820 ml retained. this is acute retention. the bladder can hold urine — station 3 storage is intact, in fact overworked. back-pressure is building from station 5 backward. the lesion is downstream of the detrusor or upstream of sensation. localization continues. decision: decompress (foley) and start localization in parallel. don't lose the diagnostic window. 3 read the cargo · ua no leukocytes, no nitrites, no blood, trace protein. clean ua. station 3 infection (cystitis) is off the table as the driver. no hematuria — no overt malignancy signal yet. the shallowest test has narrowed the differential by exclusion. off the list: uti-driven retention. 4 check the wiring · station 4 (neural) no back pain, no saddle anesthesia, no leg weakness, no recent neurologic change. diabetic but well-controlled, no neuropathy on exam. not on anticholinergics, decongestants, or opioids. neurogenic and pharmacologic causes drop off. cauda equina is not on the table — but you asked, because you would not forgive yourself for catheterizing a man with progressive cord compression and sending him home. off the list: neurogenic retention, drug-induced retention. 5 targeted history · stations 3 & 5 slow stream "for years." wakes 3× nightly. hesitancy. felt "incomplete" emptying for months. no prior catheterization, no instrumentation, no stis, no trauma. months of progressive station 5 narrowing now decompensating acutely. stricture unlikely without instrumentation or infection history. fits prostatic enlargement compressing the prostatic urethra. the lesion isn't in the lumen of station 5 — it's the wall of station 5 being squeezed from outside. working hypothesis: bph causing chronic outlet obstruction, acutely decompensated. 6 touch the lesion station · dre prostate symmetrically enlarged, smooth, rubbery, non-tender. no nodules. consistent with benign hyperplasia. no nodule means no immediate suspicion for prostate cancer at this exam — doesn't rule it out, and psa still belongs in the workup once retention is decompressed (acute retention itself elevates psa, so timing matters). no tenderness — prostatitis off the table. off the list: acute prostatitis, palpable prostate cancer. what the dre is feeling. a hyperplastic adenoma (yellow) compressing the urethral lumen and pushing up into the bladder, which has distended above it. station 5 is the lesion. station 3 is the consequence. burroughs wellcome co. 7 check upstream damage · stations 1–2 creatinine 1.4 (baseline 1.0). mild bilateral hydronephrosis. the lesion is at station 5, but back-pressure has reached station 1. high storage pressures have transmitted retrograde through stations 4, 3, 2, into the kidneys. this is why you don't treat retention as an exit problem and walk away. the upstream stations are paying the bill. decompression is renal protection, not just comfort. implication: recheck creatinine after decompression. expect post-obstructive diuresis; monitor for it. 8 lesion localized · station 5 acute urinary retention from bph. prostate compressing the urethra at station 5. upstream tracts mildly affected; renal function recoverable. five stations, eight steps, one lesion. note where you stopped — you did not need urodynamics, you did not need cystoscopy, you did not need a ct. those are the next tools if the treatment trial fails. the diagnostic depth matched the certainty you needed to act. treat at station 5. rx treatment · the layered plan decompress now. treat the lesion next. plan the definitive step. foley catheter — relieves back-pressure across the conduit. symptomatic. buys time. tamsulosin — station 4/5 smooth muscle relaxation. started immediately to improve voiding-trial odds. voiding trial in 3–7 days — test whether α-blockade alone restores function. outpatient urology follow-up — psa (delayed), discussion of 5-ari vs. surgical management (turp / holep / urolift). this is where the lesion is actually treated. renal function recheck — confirm station 1 recovers once station 5 is opened. the foley is not the treatment. it is the bridge to the treatment. what this case teaches the patient pointed at the exit. the bladder scan confirmed station 3 was distended. the lesion was at station 5. the damage had back-pressured to station 1. every station mattered, but only one was the target of the operation. ask of every patient: where did the flow first fail, and what treats it there?

label: The Conduit's Particular Pain — Referred Symptoms
text: the conduit's particular pain — referred symptoms · conduits refer in characteristic ways because back-pressure damages tissue at one station while the patient feels pain dermatomally somewhere else. you will be wrong about location constantly if you trust the patient's pointing finger. upj stone (station 1/2 boundary) → flank pain from capsular stretch of the kidney. mimics msk back pain. mid-ureteral stone (station 2) → lateral abdominal pain. mimics appendicitis on the right, diverticulitis on the left. uvj stone (station 2/3 boundary) → groin / testicular / labial pain. mimics hernia, torsion, epididymitis. bladder pathology (station 3) → suprapubic, sometimes referred to tip of penis or perineum. prostatitis (station 5-adjacent) → perineum, low back, tip of penis, testicles, rectum. five referral zones from one inflamed structure. renal capsular stretch (station 1) → flank, costovertebral angle. may radiate to ipsilateral shoulder if diaphragmatic irritation is involved. the pattern: the patient's finger points to the dermatome, not the station. same lesson you'd learn anywhere referred pain shows up. accept the symptom, distrust the localization, image the station.

label: The Algorithm, One More Time
text: the algorithm, one more time · trace upstream from where flow stops step 1 — take the exit complaint at face value. → patient says "burning," "blood," "can't go," "leaking." → that names the bucket: storage / emptying / hematuria / pain. step 2 — run the shallowest test that excludes the most. → ua + culture. bladder scan if the bucket is emptying. → a clean ua in a "uti" reroutes you. a 600 ml pvr reroutes you. step 3 — history triages the stations. → neuro symptoms? → above the conduit · station 4 wiring → slow stream for years? → chronic outlet · station 5 → painless gross hematuria? → assume malignancy until imaged + scoped step 4 — exam the lesion station directly when you can. → dre for prostate. meatal exam for st. 5 surface. → pelvic exam where appropriate. step 5 — image the station you are still blind to. → upper tract concern? → ct urogram / renal us → functional concern? → urodynamics → luminal concern? → cystoscopy step 6 — tissue only when imaging cannot answer. → biopsy is the deepest test. use last. use targeted. step 7 — treat at the lesion station. → not where it hurts. not at the exit. not at the dilation upstream. → at the station where flow first failed. may 24, 2026 — for pas, medical students, and clinical clerks the urinary tract is a conduit · trace upstream from the exit · treat at the station where flow first failed ← back to reference

label: Common Consults: The ER & Inpatient Set
text: common consults: the er & inpatient set · for the trainee taking first call. the er sees the exit complaint. your job is to localize the station where flow first failed — and to know which consults will not wait for the workup to complete.

label: The Consult Framework
text: the consult framework · every urology consult arrives in tiers (a hierarchy of clinical reasoning, separate from the anatomical stations of the conduit ). the tiers map cleanly onto how the call comes in: tier what it is who sees it 1 — presenting complaint "can't pee" · "blood in urine" · "pain" · "fever" triage nurse, er attending 2 — functional problem retention. obstructed flow. loss of continence. hemodynamic instability. er physician 3 — structural lesion stone at the uvj. prostatic enlargement. clot in the bladder. ischemic testis. you — on consult 4 — root cause bph. anticoagulation. bladder cancer. cord torsion. diabetes. urology, downstream the er physician saw tier 1 and inferred tier 2. they called you because they need someone who can localize tier 3 — the station along the urinary conduit where flow first failed — and start planning around tier 4. that is the entire consult in one sentence. what "first call" actually means you are being trusted to do three things, in order. one, identify the consults that will not survive the workup — fournier's, torsion, urosepsis with an obstructed system. two, stabilize: decompress what's distended upstream, drain what's obstructed, hold pressure where it's bleeding. three, localize the lesion well enough — to the conduit station that first failed — that the staff urologist can plan the operation by phone. the framework below exists so you do those three in the right order every time.

label: How to Read Each Consult Below
text: how to read each consult below · every entry follows the same structure. the badge tells you the clinical tempo, the clock tells you the rate at which layer failure outpaces your diagnostic window, and the lesion/symptom box localizes before the prose starts. critical

label: Example — read the structure
text: example — read the structure · clock · time-to-failure lesion site where the pathology lives e.g. cord, fascia, ureter where it presents where the patient feels it e.g. scrotum, skin, flank then: pattern · diagnostic move · treatment · when to call urology .

label: The Consults
text: the consults · urgent

label: 1. Urinary Retention
text: 1. urinary retention · clock · hours (kidneys); minutes (pain) lesion station station 5 (prostate / urethra) or wiring (control) obstructive vs neurogenic where it presents distended bladder, suprapubic pain "can't pee" — layer 1 pattern two flavors. obstructive retention : chronic outflow narrowing (bph, stricture) decompensates acutely — usually triggered by alcohol, anticholinergics, decongestants, opioids, or postoperative state. slow stream history. big bladder, big pvr. neurogenic retention : think cord — cauda equina, spinal cord injury, ms flare, diabetic autonomic neuropathy. look for saddle anesthesia, leg weakness, bowel involvement, recent back pain. acute on chronic is the most common er picture: a man with years of luts who suddenly cannot void after a hip replacement and a few opioids. diagnostic move bladder scan first — anything over 300 ml with the complaint of inability to void is retention. decompress with a foley before you finish the workup. ua + bmp. renal ultrasound if bilateral hydro is suspected or creatinine is up. always ask the neuro questions out loud — saddle, legs, bowel, back pain. if any are positive, mri lumbar spine stat, not a urology consult. treatment foley decompresses layer 2–3. tamsulosin starts immediately if obstructive — improves voiding-trial success. monitor for post-obstructive diuresis (urine output >200 ml/hr after decompression) — replace half of hourly output with ivf, recheck electrolytes. trial of void in 3–7 days as outpatient if stable. when to call urology can't pass the catheter — call before forcing. a false passage in a man with bph or stricture costs you weeks. frank hematuria with retention — likely clot retention, needs 3-way irrigation, see below. retention with sepsis or aki — admit, call now. any suspicion of cord pathology — neurosurgery first, urology second. urgent

label: 2. Renal Colic / Ureteric Stone
text: 2. renal colic / ureteric stone · clock · hours if infected; days if not lesion station station 2 — ureter (upj, pelvic brim, or uvj) three natural narrowings where it presents flank → groin → testis/labia refers along the dermatome as the stone descends pattern sudden, severe, colicky pain. patient cannot find a comfortable position — distinguishes from peritonitis, where they lie still. microscopic hematuria common, gross hematuria possible. nausea and vomiting prominent. migration of pain toward the groin/scrotum suggests descent to the uvj. diagnostic move non-contrast ct kub is the test — sees stone, sees hydro, sees alternatives (aaa, appendicitis). ua looking for hematuria and infection. cbc + bmp. if pregnant: ultrasound first, then mri if needed; ct is the last resort. treatment stones <5 mm in the distal ureter: ~80% pass. nsaids (ketorolac if renal function ok), antiemetic, tamsulosin (medical expulsive therapy — controversial but standard for distal stones >5 mm), strain urine, outpatient follow-up. stones 5–10 mm: variable. most still pass with time. outpatient urology in 1–2 weeks if uncomplicated. stones >10 mm: rarely pass spontaneously. plan for intervention (ureteroscopy / swl). when to call urology infected obstructed system — fever + stone + hydronephrosis. this is urosepsis until proven otherwise. drain immediately, see below. solitary kidney with obstruction. bilateral obstruction. aki from obstruction. intractable pain despite adequate analgesia. stone >10 mm — for elective planning, not in the ed. urgent

label: 3. Gross Hematuria
text: 3. gross hematuria · clock · hours if clot retention; days if not lesion station any station from kidney to meatus localization is the entire job where it presents color of urine — layer 1 tells you nothing about depth pattern three patterns worth distinguishing by history. initial hematuria (blood at the start, clearing) → urethral source. terminal hematuria (clear, then blood at the end) → bladder neck / trigone. total hematuria (blood throughout) → bladder or upper tract. painless gross hematuria in an adult is malignancy until proven otherwise. painful gross hematuria is usually stone or infection. anticoagulation does not cause hematuria by itself — it unmasks an underlying lesion. work it up the same way regardless. diagnostic move ua + culture (rule out infection first; treat first if positive, then re-evaluate). cbc, bmp, coags. ct urogram for the upper tracts and bladder. cystoscopy for the bladder mucosa — flexible, in clinic, the gold standard for bladder cancer detection. assess clot retention at the bedside: palpate suprapubically, bladder scan if uncertain. treatment if not retaining clot and stable: outpatient workup. if retaining clot: 3-way foley (22–24 fr, large bore) with continuous bladder irrigation (cbi). manually evacuate clots with a toomey before starting cbi — a cbi on top of a clot-filled bladder just inflates the system. reverse anticoagulation if the bleeding is consequential. type and screen, transfuse for symptomatic anemia. when to call urology clot retention requiring cbi. failure to clear clots after manual evacuation and cbi. hemodynamic instability — bleeding from anywhere in the gu tract that drops a blood pressure is a now-call. recent urologic procedure with new bleeding — assume procedure-related, see post-op section. any gross hematuria needs outpatient urology follow-up. state this explicitly in the discharge plan; do not assume the patient will find their way to clinic. critical

label: 4. Urosepsis
text: 4. urosepsis · clock · hours — mortality climbs with each one lesion station stations 1–2 — obstructed, infected upper tract closed space with bacteria where it presents fever, tachycardia, hypotension systemic — layer 1 in the worst way pattern fever + flank pain + hydronephrosis = obstructed pyelonephritis until proven otherwise. diabetic, immunosuppressed, elderly, recently instrumented, or with a known stone are the at-risk groups. they can look surprisingly well one hour and crash the next. an infected, obstructed system is a closed-space infection. antibiotics alone will not drain it. pressure has to come off. what an obstructed, infected kidney looks like inside. purulent exudate fills the collecting system; abscesses are scattered through the cortex; the parenchyma is inflamed. drainage is not optional. antibiotics arrive through the bloodstream; they do not enter the closed obstructed space. from burroughs wellcome co., atlas of the urinary tract . diagnostic move lactate, blood cultures, ua + urine culture, cbc, bmp, coags. ct abdomen/pelvis with contrast (or non-contrast if aki) to identify the obstruction. sepsis bundle: fluids, broad-spectrum antibiotics, source control. do not wait for cultures to start antibiotics — but understand that antibiotics are the second move, not the first. treatment drainage before antibiotics will work. two options: ureteral stent — urology, retrograde, in the or. faster if available. percutaneous nephrostomy — ir, antegrade, at the bedside or in ir suite. often the right call after hours. the choice is local — whoever can drain faster. both work. the wrong answer is "let's see if the antibiotics work." when to call urology always, immediately, in parallel with calling ir. this is the consult where the phone call goes out before the ct result is back if the picture is clear enough. icu admission expected. critical

label: 5. Fournier's Gangrene
text: 5. fournier's gangrene · clock · time to or is the only variable lesion station fascial planes of the perineum (layer geometry, not conduit) necrotizing infection along colles' / buck's / dartos where it presents scrotal / perineal skin often deceptively unimpressive early if you suspect fournier's, stop reading and call. pain out of proportion to exam findings in the perineum, scrotum, or groin — especially in a diabetic, obese, or immunosuppressed patient — is fournier's until proven otherwise. skin findings lag the fascial necrosis by hours. crepitus is late. hemodynamic instability is later. time to operative debridement is the single variable that correlates with survival. imaging delays the or. antibiotics do not penetrate dead tissue. cut. pattern diabetic, immunosuppressed, alcoholic, perirectal abscess, recent perineal procedure, or chronic catheter. severe pain in the perineum or scrotum. skin can look like cellulitis early — pink, warm, swollen — then progress to dusky, purple, bullous, finally black. crepitus is highly specific but not sensitive. foul-smelling, "dishwater" purulent discharge if open. systemic toxicity disproportionate to skin findings. diagnostic move this is clinical. if you are sure, ct is a delay you cannot afford. if you are uncertain and the patient is hemodynamically stable, ct can confirm fascial gas and define extent — but a normal ct does not rule it out. lrinec score is supportive, not diagnostic. labs (cbc, bmp, lactate, crp, glucose, coags) draw on the way to the or. treatment broad-spectrum antibiotics with anaerobic coverage (e.g. pip-tazo + clindamycin + vancomycin, per local protocol). start in the ed. aggressive resuscitation. icu disposition. operative debridement. surgical debridement is the treatment. everything else is supportive. expect repeat trips to the or every 24–48 hours until clean. suprapubic catheter or diverting colostomy may be required depending on extent. when to call urology now. before any of the above. call urology and general surgery together — the debridement crosses anatomic territories. critical

label: 6. Priapism
text: 6. priapism · clock · 4 h functional; 24 h is too late lesion station corpus cavernosum — ischemic outflow failure or arterial fistula in non-ischemic where it presents painful, rigid penis >4 hours glans typically soft, corpora rigid pattern two types, totally different problems. ischemic (low-flow, veno-occlusive) is a compartment syndrome of the corpora. painful, fully rigid, dark blood on aspiration. causes: sickle cell, leukemia, intracavernosal injections, pde5 inhibitors, trazodone, cocaine. treat as an emergency. non-ischemic (high-flow, arterial) follows perineal trauma. partially rigid, painless, bright red blood on aspiration. not an emergency — managed semi-electively with selective embolization. diagnostic move history (sickle cell? trauma? drugs?). cavernosal blood gas distinguishes — dark and acidotic = ischemic; bright and arterial = non-ischemic. doppler ultrasound is confirmatory if uncertain. treatment — ischemic aspirate the corpus cavernosum with a 19g needle through the glans or lateral shaft. remove dark blood. irrigate with saline. inject phenylephrine — dilute to 100–500 mcg/ml, 1 ml aliquots every 3–5 minutes, monitoring bp and hr. maximum dose ~1 mg total in most protocols. if >36 hours or refractory: surgical shunt. permanent ed risk rises with every hour after the 4-hour mark. treatment — non-ischemic conservative initially. many resolve. selective pudendal artery embolization if persistent. not a same-day procedure. when to call urology for any priapism >4 hours, call. if it's ischemic, you want urology hands-on for the aspiration and injection. if it's non-ischemic, you still want them planning the embolization conversation with ir. critical

label: 7. Testicular Torsion
text: 7. testicular torsion · clock · 6 h to save the testis lesion station spermatic cord — vascular occlusion (network geometry — testicular artery) testicular artery strangled by the twist where it presents acute scrotal pain often radiating to the lower abdomen pattern adolescent or young adult, sudden severe scrotal pain, often with nausea and vomiting. sometimes wakes from sleep. testis high-riding, transverse lie, absent cremasteric reflex. pain not relieved by elevation (prehn's sign negative — differentiates from epididymitis where elevation helps, although do not stake the diagnosis on prehn alone). can present atypically in younger children and post-pubertal men — never bet against torsion in a sudden-onset acute scrotum. diagnostic move clinical. doppler ultrasound shows decreased or absent testicular blood flow — but do not delay surgery for ultrasound if the clinical picture is convincing. the clock starts when the cord twists, not when the patient arrives. treatment or for emergent scrotal exploration, detorsion, and bilateral orchiopexy. manual detorsion in the ed ("opening the book" — rotate the affected testis outward, away from midline) can be attempted while preparing for or — relieves pain, may restore flow, does not replace the operation. bilateral orchiopexy because the contralateral side has the same anatomic predisposition (bell-clapper deformity). when to call urology immediately upon suspicion. npo, mark the patient for or. salvage rate is >90% at <6 hours, <10% at >24 hours. the consult is not "rule out torsion" — it's "this is torsion until urology rules it out in the or." urgent

label: 8. Traumatic GU Injury
text: 8. traumatic gu injury · clock · mechanism-dependent lesion station stations 1–5 — any conduit station, mechanism-dependent force vector predicts depth where it presents hematuria, perineal bruising, blood at meatus small surface signs, big underlying injury renal trauma blunt — usually mvc, sports impact, fall. penetrating — gunshot or stab. hematuria does not correlate with severity; a major renal pedicle injury can have minimal hematuria. ct abdomen/pelvis with iv contrast (delayed phase for collecting system) grades the injury (aast i–v). most blunt low-grade injuries (i–iii) managed non-operatively. active extravasation or hemodynamic instability → angio-embolization or or. watch for delayed bleeding from a pseudoaneurysm — readmit threshold low. bladder trauma pelvic fracture + gross hematuria = bladder injury until proven otherwise. ct cystogram (retrograde fill with contrast, then image) is the test — not a contrasted ct alone, which underdiagnoses by 50%. two patterns: extraperitoneal (most common, with pelvic fracture) — managed with foley drainage 10–14 days, repeat cystogram before catheter removal. intraperitoneal (blunt trauma to a full bladder, classically a seatbelt injury) — surgical repair. always. urethral trauma blood at the meatus is a stop sign. do not place a foley until urethral injury is ruled out. two patterns: posterior (pelvic fracture, "high-riding prostate" on dre — though that finding is unreliable) → retrograde urethrogram (rug); often managed with suprapubic catheter and delayed repair. anterior (straddle injury) → rug; may be primarily repaired or stented depending on the lesion. when to call urology any blood at the meatus before catheterization. gross hematuria in a trauma patient. pelvic fracture with any gu sign. penetrating gu trauma — always operative until proven otherwise. renal injury aast grade iii or higher. urgent

label: 9. Post-Operative Complications
text: 9. post-operative complications · clock · variable; treat the new finding seriously lesion station whatever station was operated on prostate, kidney, bladder, ureter where it presents drain output, urine color, fever, ileus all of which can mean several things hematuria post-turp some bleeding is expected — pink urine, mild clots, clearing over 24–48 hours. bright red, brisk, or with clots that overwhelm the cbi is not expected. increase irrigation rate, manually evacuate clots, check coags, check that the catheter balloon isn't pulled into the prostatic fossa applying tamponade incorrectly. persistent bleeding → return to or for fulguration. urine leak post-partial nephrectomy / post-pyeloplasty high drain output that smells like urine, or drain creatinine markedly higher than serum creatinine, confirms urine leak. most heal with prolonged drainage and a ureteral stent to lower the pressure in the collecting system (decompress upstream so the leak closes downstream). persistent leak → percutaneous nephrostomy + stent. reoperation rare. catheter pulled / fell out post-prostatectomy or post-urethroplasty do not blindly replace. call urology. a urethral catheter passing through a fresh anastomosis can disrupt it. often requires cystoscopic placement over a wire. treat this as a same-night call. anuria post-cystectomy with ileal conduit stoma not draining. first — is the stoma viable (pink)? dusky stoma is a vascular emergency. then — check the catheters in the conduit; mucus plug is the usual cause. irrigate the conduit catheters gently. if still no output, imaging of the ureteroenteric anastomosis. ileus + fever + leukocytosis post-pelvic urologic surgery think urinoma, abscess, ureteric injury. ct abdomen/pelvis with delayed images. the clinical picture you do not want is "anastomotic leak masquerading as ileus." when to call urology any unexpected finding in the first 30 days after a urologic operation belongs on the operating surgeon's phone, not just on a generic urology consult. the surgeon knows what their reconstruction looks like and what should and should not be happening.

label: The Triage Matrix
text: the triage matrix · the principle: the rate of layer failure determines tempo. if the lesion advances faster than your diagnostic window, you act first and confirm later. if it advances slower, you image and plan. critical · act now fournier's gangrene — or clock testicular torsion — 6 h clock urosepsis with obstruction — drain first ischemic priapism — >4 h harms penetrating gu trauma hemodynamic instability from any gu source failure outpaces workup urgent · stabilize & localize acute retention — decompress, then plan colic with fever — verify not obstructed gross hematuria with clot retention pyelonephritis without obstruction blunt renal trauma grade iii+ post-op complications within 30 days act this shift semi-elective · disposition stable ureteric stone <5 mm, no fever, no aki asymptomatic microscopic hematuria chronic retention , stable creatinine, no overflow uncomplicated cystitis non-ischemic priapism (rare to see at all) stable grade i–ii renal trauma outpatient or routine the framing question for every consult, ask: what is the rate of layer failure, and does it outpace my diagnostic window? if yes → act now, confirm later. if no → confirm, then act. this is the one habit that separates a useful first-call from one that holds the operation up.

label: When to Wake Up the Urologist
text: when to wake up the urologist · the pa needs rules, not judgment. use these. they are intentionally over-inclusive on the side of waking someone up. wake them up. right now. suspected fournier's gangrene . no imaging required. suspected testicular torsion within the operative window. obstructed pyelonephritis — fever + hydronephrosis + sepsis physiology. ischemic priapism >4 hours. gu bleeding with hemodynamic instability . penetrating gu trauma. failed catheterization in acute retention with rising creatinine or sepsis . any post-operative urology patient within 30 days with a new acute finding. call within the shift, not at 0300. successful foley placement for retention — patient stable, no aki. sign-out call is fine. renal colic, controlled pain, no fever, no aki, stone passable. gross hematuria, no clot retention, stable hemoglobin, anticoagulation reviewed. stable grade i–ii renal trauma. uncomplicated uti in a normal host. handle yourself; pass to clinic. asymptomatic microscopic hematuria with a clean ua and no risk factors — workup as outpatient. stable chronic retention without overflow incontinence or aki. phimosis without obstruction. non-painful scrotal masses (with documentation and follow-up arranged). the default when in doubt, call. the cost of an unnecessary call is one annoyed urologist. the cost of a missed fournier's or torsion is a patient who loses tissue or dies. the asymmetry is not subtle. you will lose nothing in the long run by being the pa who called at 0300 about a possible torsion that turned out to be epididymitis. you will lose a lot by being the one who didn't.

label: A Worked Consult
text: a worked consult · pod1 after right total hip replacement. 68-year-old man. has not voided since the foley came out 6 hours ago. er is involved because the floor team is uncertain. call comes to you. 1 what the er / floor sees · tier 1 "post-op patient can't void." floor team has the surface complaint and a generic post-op picture. no vitals abnormality yet. they want to know if they can replace the catheter, or whether something else is going on. the floor's instinct is to put the foley back. your instinct should be to localize before you instrument. 2 what you assess first · tier 2 bladder scan. vitals. med list. scan: 720 ml. confirms retention — the symptom is real and the bladder is the reservoir, not just a sensation issue. vitals: hr 88, bp 138/82, afebrile. no sepsis. medications since or: spinal anesthetic, fentanyl pca, ketorolac, ondansetron, oxybutynin (someone wrote it for "bladder spasms" pre-op and it never came off). the pca and oxybutynin are the immediate functional cause. underneath them may sit chronic bph. working frame: drug-precipitated acute retention, almost certainly on a background of luts. 3 targeted history · tier 3 (structural lesion) "have you had trouble peeing before this?" yes. slow stream for two years. nocturia twice a night. has been "meaning to see somebody about my prostate." never had retention before. no back pain, no leg weakness, no saddle anesthesia, no bowel change. no prior catheterization, no instrumentation. this is acute-on-chronic outflow obstruction precipitated by anticholinergic + opioid load + supine recovery, with a layer 5 substrate (prostate). off the list: cord pathology. on the list: bph unmasked. 4 decompress · relieve back-pressure foley, 16 fr, sterile, single attempt. passes without difficulty. 780 ml out over the first 15 minutes. no gross hematuria. patient instantly comfortable. you are now in the diagnostic window the catheter bought you — do not waste it. order: bmp, ua, urine culture (it'll grow eventually if anything; better to know now). confirm afebrile, watch for the next hour. result: creatinine 1.1 (baseline 1.0). ua clean. no infection. no aki worth a consult on its own. 5 what urology needs to know · tier 4 (root cause) the 30-second handoff. when you call the staff urologist or the resident, give them this, in this order: "68 y/o man, pod1 right tha, acute retention 720 ml on scan." "two-year history of luts, never retained before. no neuro symptoms." "foley passed easily, drained 780 ml, no hematuria." "bmp normal, ua clean, afebrile." "on pca and oxybutynin — i'd like to stop the oxybutynin and start tamsulosin." "plan: leave foley for 48–72 hours, trial of void after offending meds are off, urology clinic follow-up." that is the entire consult. you have localized, decompressed, named the root cause, and offered a plan. the urologist either agrees and goes back to sleep, or modifies the plan by one variable. either way you have done your job. 6 management · station 5 (urethral outlet) foley + tamsulosin + stop the offenders. discontinue oxybutynin. de-escalate pca to prn oral opioid plus scheduled acetaminophen. start tamsulosin 0.4 mg nightly. foley to leg bag, training the patient and family on care. document: "acute urinary retention post-arthroplasty, multifactorial — anticholinergic, opioid, supine state — on a background of clinical bph. decompressed. tov planned in 48–72 hours after offending agents discontinued. outpatient urology referral placed." 7 the disposition hinge · what you are watching for the tov decides everything. trial of void in 48–72 hours. three outcomes, three dispositions: voids well, low pvr (<150 ml) → home on tamsulosin, outpatient urology in 4–6 weeks. discharged when ortho clears him. voids partially, pvr 200–400 ml → judgment. often replace catheter, try again in a week as outpatient. tamsulosin continued. fails to void, or pvr >400 ml → foley with leg bag home, outpatient urology in 1–2 weeks, frank conversation about bph workup and likely intervention (medical vs surgical). the hospital stay is no longer about urology — orthopedics owns the disposition for the hip. urology's contribution is the bladder plan that travels home with him. what this consult teaches you did not need urodynamics. you did not need cystoscopy. you did not need to wake anyone up. you localized the lesion (layer 5, prostate), identified the precipitant (layer 4, drug-induced), decompressed the functional problem (layer 2, retention), and handed off a plan that survives discharge. most of your consults will look like this. the point of all the other entries in this document is so that you recognize the ones that don't — and call accordingly. written may 24, 2026 — for a pa student taking first call the framework before the cases, so the cases make sense when they arrive. layer by layer, surface to source. ← back to reference

label: Procedures the Trainee Will Do
text: procedures the trainee will do · the bedside set for pas, medical students, and clinical clerks. foley, suprapubic tap, urine collection, bladder irrigation, stoma assessment. step-by-step with the failure modes attached — because the failures are when you'll need to think, not when you're following the steps.

label: The Operating Principle
text: the operating principle · every procedure below has three failure modes you need to recognize: the technical failure (it didn't work), the wrong-procedure failure (you did the right steps for the wrong indication), and the missed-finding failure (the procedure worked but the result tells you something you didn't expect to hear). the steps are the easy part. the failure modes are why you read this twice. before you do anything sterile confirm: right patient, right side, right indication, allergies (especially latex and chlorhexidine), anticoagulation status, mental capacity to consent. document the indication in the chart before you do the procedure, not after. "i did a foley because the patient was retaining 800 ml on scan after failing to void for 8 hours" is a different note from "foley placed per request." 1

label: Foley Catheter Placement
text: foley catheter placement · station 5 → 3 · sterile indication acute urinary retention (decompression). accurate output monitoring in a critically ill patient. healing of a urinary tract injury or fresh anastomosis. procedural — perioperative drainage where prolonged surgery is expected. contraindication / call before you try blood at the meatus in a trauma patient. stop. get a retrograde urethrogram. suspected urethral disruption (pelvic fracture, high-riding prostate). recent urethral reconstruction or fresh anastomosis — call the operating surgeon. known stricture disease that has previously required cystoscopic guidance. kit (16 fr is the default for men and women) 16 fr 2-way foley (coudé tip — see below — for the difficult male). sterile gloves, drapes, prep solution, 10 ml syringe of sterile water, lubricant (lidocaine jelly preferred for men), drainage bag. bladder scanner, ideally, before and after. standard technique — male position supine, expose. prep with the dominant hand contaminated as soon as you hold the penis — use the other hand for the catheter. hold the penis perpendicular to the body. instill 10 ml of lidocaine jelly into the urethra , hold meatus closed for 2 minutes — this is not optional, it relaxes the external sphincter and patients tolerate the catheter without it badly. advance the catheter steadily to the hub. most resistance is at the membranous urethra (external sphincter) — ask the patient to take a slow breath in, sphincter relaxes. confirm urine return before inflating. if no urine returns and you have not reached the hub, do not inflate. inflate the balloon with 10 ml sterile water. gently retract until the balloon meets the bladder neck. standard technique — female position supine, knees flexed, frog-legged. identify the urethral meatus — between the clitoris and vaginal introitus. in post-menopausal or obese patients it can be retracted; use the index and middle fingers of the non-dominant hand to spread the labia and visualize. prep, lubricate, advance until urine returns or to the hub. if the catheter goes into the vagina, leave it there as a marker and pass a second catheter above it into the urethra. then remove the misplaced one. failure modes no urine on initial pass. if the bladder scan was >200 ml, the catheter is in a false passage or coiled. do not inflate. withdraw, irrigate the catheter to confirm patency, retry with more lube and more time. hematuria during passage. stop. means you've created a false passage or scraped a stricture. call urology. resistance at the prostate. the most common stopping point in older men. see the decision tree below. balloon won't inflate. catheter is in the urethra, not the bladder. do not force. withdraw and reposition. patient in severe pain mid-procedure. stop. reassess. often a stricture or false passage. the difficult male catheterization — decision tree 1. standard 16 fr fails at the prostate. → try a coudé tip 16 fr coudé. the curved tip points up (toward the patient's belly) — keep the indicator stripe pointing at 12 o'clock during advancement. this rides over the median lobe of the prostate. most bph-related failures end here. 2. coudé fails. → upsize, don't downsize counterintuitive. a larger catheter (18 or 20 fr) is stiffer and rides over the prostate more reliably than a 14 fr, which kinks. downsizing is for strictures, not bph. if you cannot tell stricture from bph, stop and call. 3. two passes have failed, or there is fresh blood. → stop. call urology. the pa's rule: two failed attempts is the ceiling. a third attempt creates more false passages and more bleeding. urology will place over a wire under cystoscopic guidance. that preserves the urethra for future catheterizations. a third blind attempt does not. 4. the bladder is dangerously full and urology cannot come immediately. → percutaneous suprapubic tap as a bridge see procedure #2. decompresses the bladder while urology mobilizes. not a definitive solution. document the bridge clearly. removal deflate the balloon by drawing back with the syringe — do not cut the inflation port. withdraw gently. if you meet resistance on deflation, the balloon may not have fully decompressed (check by attempting to overinflate, then deflate again). persistent failure to deflate is a call to urology, not a yank. 2

label: Suprapubic Bladder Aspiration / Percutaneous Decompression
text: suprapubic bladder aspiration / percutaneous decompression · station 1 ↔ 3 · sterile indication urgent decompression when urethral catheterization has failed and urology is delayed. sterile urine sample in an infant or non-cooperative patient where catheter sample is impractical (less common in adults). contraindication empty or unidentifiable bladder on scan — confirm volume first. coagulopathy, anticoagulation — relative; weigh against retention severity. prior lower abdominal surgery with possible adherent bowel. suprapubic skin infection at the insertion site. technique (single-stick decompression) confirm bladder volume on scan (≥300 ml ideal — gives you a target). identify insertion: 2 fingerbreadths above the symphysis pubis, midline. prep, drape, local anesthetic with lidocaine through the planned tract. advance an 18g spinal needle perpendicular to the skin, aspirating as you go. urine return confirms intravesical position. for sample only: collect, withdraw, dress. for decompression bridge: use a dedicated suprapubic catheter kit per institutional protocol. failure modes no urine return. either the bladder is not where you thought (re-scan) or you are too superficial. do not redirect blindly; reconfirm landmarks. frank blood return. withdraw. likely vessel rather than bladder; rescan and reposition. stool / cloudy non-urine fluid. bowel injury until proven otherwise. withdraw, broad-spectrum antibiotics, call surgery. the decompression that isn't yours alone a formal indwelling suprapubic catheter — not just an aspiration — is a urologic procedure. the single-stick aspiration described above is a temporizing maneuver, used when a patient is in distress and an indwelling solution is minutes away. place an aspiration; arrange an indwelling tube. document the gap explicitly. 3

label: Urine Collection for Culture
text: urine collection for culture · station 5 / cargo · sample integrity is everything why the technique matters a poorly collected sample is worse than no sample. it generates a false positive — bacteria from the perineum colonize the cup — and you end up treating a culture, not a patient. the most common urology error on the consult service is treating asymptomatic bacteriuria in a contaminated specimen. methods, in order of cleanliness method clean enough for caveat clean-catch midstream symptomatic outpatient women, men with intact prepuce coach the technique — most failures are technique failures, not biology. straight catheter sample hospitalized patients, anyone who cannot midstream one-time pass. discard the first 5 ml (urethral flora). collect from the stream. catheter port aspiration patients with indwelling foley sample from the port, not the bag . bag urine is colonized within hours regardless of patient. suprapubic aspiration definitive — used clinically rarely in adults the gold standard. any growth is real. how to read what comes back >10⁵ cfu/ml of a single organism with pyuria + symptoms → uti, treat. >10⁵ cfu/ml, no pyuria, no symptoms → asymptomatic bacteriuria. do not treat outside of pregnancy and pre-urologic-instrumentation cases. treating it selects resistance and changes nothing. mixed flora, two or three organisms → contaminated sample. recollect with better technique before deciding anything. pyuria with no growth → think atypicals, prior antibiotic, urolithiasis with inflammation, tuberculosis if subacute, or interstitial cystitis. the teaching point a urine culture is a question, not an answer. the answer comes from matching the culture to the patient . bacteria in a cup with no fever, no dysuria, no flank pain is colonization. bacteria in a cup with sepsis is urosepsis. same lab result. different diseases. 4

label: Continuous Bladder Irrigation (CBI) Setup
text: continuous bladder irrigation (cbi) setup · station 3 · clot management indication gross hematuria with clot retention. post-turp / post-prostatectomy / post-cystoscopy with significant bleeding. prevention of clot formation in expected bleeding (immediately post-turp). kit 3-way foley — 22 or 24 fr. the third lumen is the inflow channel. a 3-way foley is non-negotiable; you cannot run cbi through a 2-way. sterile saline irrigation bags (3 l) ×2 minimum, with y-tubing. drainage bag — large capacity, urometer if monitoring closely. toomey or piston syringe, 60 ml, sterile saline for manual evacuation. setup, in order manual clot evacuation first. disconnect the drainage tubing. use a toomey syringe with sterile saline. inject 60 ml, withdraw, repeat until the return is pink, not red. you cannot irrigate continuously through a bladder full of clot — the fluid pools above the clot and the bladder distends without flow. hang the irrigation bag, prime the inflow line. connect inflow to the third port of the foley. connect outflow to the drainage bag. start irrigation at a rate that keeps the effluent pink, not red . titrate up if the effluent darkens; titrate down once clear. track input and output meticulously. net output should exceed net input — this is your reassurance that flow is forward. failure modes bladder distends, no outflow. clot has occluded the eyelets of the catheter. stop the inflow. manually evacuate clots. restart only when patency is confirmed. bright red effluent persists or worsens. bleeding is outpacing your irrigation. increase rate, manually evacuate again, check coags, call urology. patient with abdominal pain and tense suprapubic fullness on cbi. distension. stop the irrigation immediately. assume clot occlusion until proven otherwise. you can rupture a bladder this way. negative net output (more in than out over the shift). stop, evacuate, re-set. document. the cbi rule cbi is not a substitute for clot evacuation; it is a maintenance system after the bladder is clear. starting cbi on top of a clot-filled bladder makes things worse. evacuate, then irrigate. 5

label: Urostomy / Ileal Conduit Stoma Assessment
text: urostomy / ileal conduit stoma assessment · station 3 substitute · vascular check what you're assessing for stoma viability — is the bowel mucosa pink and healthy? output — volume, character, presence of mucus (expected) versus frank blood or pus (not). skin around the stoma — appropriate fit of the appliance, no leakage, no excoriation. patency — anuria from a conduit is a workup, not a wait. the 30-second exam color. healthy stoma is beefy pink to red. pale pink is okay. dusky purple is ischemia. black is necrosis. both are calls now. moisture. mucosa should look glistening. dry, dull, or shriveled is a problem. edema. some swelling for the first 6 weeks post-op is normal. increasing edema later is not. retraction or prolapse. stoma flush or below skin level after maturation is retraction. stoma protruding several cm is prolapse. both prompt urology review, neither is usually emergent. output. a healthy adult ileal conduit produces 1–2 l/day of urine with mucus strands. no output in 4–6 hours, with the stoma viable, is a workup — irrigate the conduit catheters gently with saline (mucus plug is common) and call if no return. failure modes dusky stoma. vascular emergency. operating team needs to know within minutes. do not wait for "it might pink up." no output, viable stoma. irrigate gently. if still nothing, image (ct) to assess the ureteroenteric anastomosis and the conduit lumen. frank blood from the stoma. either intraluminal (bleeding from inside the conduit or upstream) or extrinsic (skin trauma from the appliance). inspect; if intraluminal, call. skin breakdown around the stoma. appliance fit problem. the wound ostomy nurse is your friend; this is in their territory. the mental model for ileal conduit an ileal conduit is a short segment of small bowel that has been disconnected from the gi tract and reconnected to the ureters at one end, with the other end brought out as a stoma. it is not a bladder. it does not store. it passively drains. urine has bowel mucus in it (normal) and is mildly more acidic and salty than ordinary urine. anuria in a conduit patient is a mechanical problem until proven otherwise — mucus plug, kinked appliance, anastomotic issue — not a renal problem.

label: The Common Thread
text: the common thread · each procedure above has the same shape: a defined indication, an absolute contraindication, a sequence of steps, and a failure mode where the procedure no longer belongs to you. the expert is not the person who never fails — it is the person who recognizes the failure two steps earlier than the novice and stops. three rules that survive every procedure two failed attempts is the ceiling. any procedure. the third attempt creates the complication that becomes the consult. if you cannot name the failure mode you're at, you do not understand the procedure well enough to continue. stop. reread. restart or escalate. document the indication before, not after. if the procedure goes wrong, the note that says "placed because creatinine rising on retention of 800 ml" reads very differently from "placed per order." written may 24, 2026 — for pas, medical students, and clinical clerks ← back to reference

label: Medications & Their Stations
text: medications & their stations · every urology drug acts at a specific station along the urinary conduit through a specific receptor. learn the receptor and the station; the rest follows. memorize the drug without the mechanism and you will reach for the wrong agent in the right scenario.

label: The Map
text: the map · five conduit stations from production to expulsion (see the urinary tract ), four drug classes that matter for storage/voiding, and two extras that show up constantly. here is where each acts: kidney filtration, concentration 1 antibiotics (concentration matters), desmopressin (concentrating) where the urine is made; where some antibiotics achieve their working concentration. ureter smooth muscle, peristalsis 2 α1-blockers (met — medical expulsive therapy) relaxation of distal ureter facilitates stone passage. bladder (detrusor) m3 (contract), β3 (relax) 3 antimuscarinics, β3 agonists, intravesical bont overactive bladder — quiet the detrusor or relax it. bladder neck / internal sphincter α1a-rich 4 α1-blockers (bph symptoms, voiding trial) open the smooth-muscle gate to allow flow. urethra / prostate α1a smooth muscle + glandular tissue 5 α1-blockers (dynamic), 5α-reductase inhibitors (static), pde5 the two components of bph: smooth-muscle tone and gland volume — two drug classes, two targets. the cargo · urine itself not a station — what flows through the conduit c phenazopyridine (urinary analgesic) numbs the symptom in the cargo. treats nothing. useful, and dangerous when mistaken for definitive therapy. the frame when you see a urology medication on a chart, ask three questions. which station is the lesion at? which station does this drug act at? do those match? tamsulosin for retention from bph: lesion at station 5, drug at stations 4–5, match. oxybutynin for retention from bph: lesion at station 5, drug at station 3 — the drug will quiet the bladder that's already failing to empty. mismatch. you just made it worse.

label: The Drug Classes
text: the drug classes · s 4·5·2

label: α1-Adrenergic Blockers
text: α1-adrenergic blockers · α1a (prostate, bladder neck) · α1b (vasculature) · α1d (ureter, detrusor) smooth muscle relaxers mechanism block α1-adrenergic receptors → smooth muscle relaxation. the clinically useful effect is at α1a (prostate/bladder neck → opens the outlet). the unwanted effect is at α1b (vasculature → vasodilation → hypotension). newer "uroselective" agents (tamsulosin, silodosin) target α1a preferentially; older ones (terazosin, doxazosin) hit α1b as well and drop blood pressure. indications bph-associated luts — first-line, mainstay. voiding trial after acute retention — improves the odds of a successful trial of void. medical expulsive therapy (met) for distal ureteric stones — best evidence for stones >5 mm at the uvj. female voiding dysfunction from primary bladder neck obstruction — off-label but used. agents drug dose notes tamsulosin 0.4 mg po daily (after a meal) workhorse. α1a-selective. floppy-iris syndrome — flag for the ophthalmologist before cataract surgery. silodosin 8 mg po daily most α1a-selective. most retrograde ejaculation. least bp drop. alfuzosin 10 mg po daily less selective; tolerable bp profile. terazosin / doxazosin titrated up over weeks older, also antihypertensive. useful when you want both effects; otherwise tamsulosin is cleaner. side effects to flag orthostatic hypotension — first-dose phenomenon, worst with non-selective agents. take at bedtime. retrograde ejaculation — α1a-mediated bladder neck relaxation. common with tamsulosin, very common with silodosin. tell the patient before they take the first dose. intraoperative floppy iris syndrome (ifis) — applies to all but worst with tamsulosin. patients facing cataract surgery should tell their ophthalmologist. dizziness, nasal congestion, fatigue. the teaching point α1-blockers are dynamic — they relax tone. they don't shrink anything. effect is felt in days. if you stop them, the symptom returns within days. pair with a 5-ari when the prostate is large and the patient wants a chance at long-term independence from the medication. s 5

label: 5α-Reductase Inhibitors (5-ARIs)
text: 5α-reductase inhibitors (5-aris) · block conversion of testosterone → dihydrotestosterone (dht) prostate shrinkers mechanism dht is the androgen that drives prostatic glandular growth. 5α-reductase converts testosterone to dht. block the enzyme → dht falls by 70–90% → prostate volume falls by ~20–25% over 6–12 months. this is the static component of bph. indications bph with a large prostate (>40 ml on imaging or dre) — where actual gland reduction matters. reduction of acute urinary retention and bph-related surgery risk over the long term (combat, mtops data). hematuria of prostatic origin — reduces vascularity. off-label: androgenic alopecia (finasteride 1 mg). agents drug dose notes finasteride 5 mg po daily blocks type ii 5α-reductase. dutasteride 0.5 mg po daily blocks types i and ii. slightly greater dht suppression; clinically similar. side effects to flag sexual — decreased libido, ed, ejaculatory volume reduction. counsel before starting. gynecomastia, breast tenderness. psa reduction by ~50% after 6–12 months. always double the psa when interpreting in a patient on a 5-ari, and document the multiplier in the chart. failure to do this is a classic missed cancer. pregnancy hazard — crushed or broken tablets, or semen exposure during sex in some cases, can cause genital ambiguity in a male fetus. women of childbearing potential should not handle crushed tablets. the teaching point 5-aris are structural — they shrink. effect is felt in months. the right patient is the one with the big prostate who can wait six months. the wrong patient is the man in acute retention asking what will help him void tomorrow — give him tamsulosin for that, and add a 5-ari if his prostate is large enough to benefit over time. tamsulosin is the now-drug. finasteride/dutasteride is the year-drug. s 3

label: Antimuscarinics (Anticholinergics)
