text: antimuscarinics (anticholinergics) · m3 antagonism — detrusor (and salivary, gi, eye, brain) detrusor quieters mechanism the detrusor contracts via m3 muscarinic receptors. block them → fewer involuntary contractions → less urgency, less frequency, fewer urge-incontinence episodes. the cost is everywhere else muscarinic receptors live: salivary glands (dry mouth), gut (constipation), eye (blurry vision, raised iop in angle-closure glaucoma), brain (cognition). indications overactive bladder (oab) — urgency, frequency, urge incontinence — without significant outflow obstruction or retention. neurogenic detrusor overactivity — often higher doses. agents drug receptor selectivity notes oxybutynin (ir / er / transdermal) non-selective m1–m5 cheapest, most side effects. crosses bbb — worst for cognition. avoid in elderly. tolterodine non-selective but bladder-preferential better tolerated than oxybutynin ir. solifenacin m3-selective once daily. cleaner profile. darifenacin m3-selective less cns penetration; preferred in older patients if antimuscarinic is needed. trospium quaternary amine — minimal bbb crossing cognitive-sparing option. side effects to flag urinary retention. the same mechanism that quiets the detrusor in overactivity can paralyze it in someone with marginal voiding. do not give to a man with significant bph and high pvr. anticholinergic burden — sums with other anticholinergics (tcas, diphenhydramine, antipsychotics). the elderly patient on three of them is a delirium waiting for the trigger. acute angle-closure glaucoma — contraindicated. open-angle glaucoma is fine. cognitive decline — emerging long-term data, especially with oxybutynin. prefer β3 agonists in older patients when you can. the trap a man with frequency and urgency from bph-induced overflow can look like oab. treating him with an antimuscarinic before checking a pvr will tip him into frank retention. always check a pvr before starting an antimuscarinic in a man with luts. if the pvr is >150 ml, you're treating the wrong layer. s 3

label: β3-Adrenergic Agonists
text: β3-adrenergic agonists · β3 stimulation — detrusor smooth muscle relaxation (storage phase) detrusor relaxers mechanism the detrusor expresses β3-adrenergic receptors that, when stimulated, promote relaxation during the storage phase. activating them increases bladder capacity and decreases urgency without paralyzing detrusor contraction during voiding — which is the win over antimuscarinics. indications overactive bladder — alternative to antimuscarinics, especially in patients with cognitive concerns, dry mouth burden, or contraindications. combination therapy with an antimuscarinic for refractory oab (synergistic). agents drug dose notes mirabegron 25–50 mg po daily the original. modest bp elevation — check baseline bp, recheck after starting. vibegron 75 mg po daily newer; minimal bp signal. side effects hypertension — modest, but real with mirabegron. avoid in uncontrolled htn. headache, nasopharyngitis, uti symptoms. drug interactions — mirabegron is a cyp2d6 inhibitor; raises metoprolol, some antidepressants. why this class exists antimuscarinics treat oab by paralyzing the detrusor everywhere. β3 agonists treat oab by relaxing the detrusor during storage without blocking its ability to contract for voiding. cleaner mechanism, cleaner side effect profile. they are the right starting choice in many older patients now, even though antimuscarinics remain cheaper and more familiar. s 5

label: PDE5 Inhibitors
text: pde5 inhibitors · block phosphodiesterase 5 → increased cgmp → vascular smooth muscle relaxation vascular relaxers mechanism nitric oxide → cgmp → smooth muscle relaxation. pde5 degrades cgmp. block pde5, cgmp rises, smooth muscle relaxes — specifically, the smooth muscle of the corpus cavernosum (erections), the prostate and bladder neck (bph symptoms), and the pulmonary vasculature (pulmonary hypertension). indications erectile dysfunction — first-line oral therapy. bph-related luts (tadalafil 5 mg daily) — particularly when ed and bph coexist. pulmonary hypertension (different dosing, different formulation). agents drug onset / duration notes sildenafil 30–60 min onset, ~4 h duration take on empty stomach. bluish vision tinge (pde6 cross-reactivity). tadalafil 30 min, 24–36 h ("weekender") 5 mg daily for bph ± ed. once-daily dosing is the luts use. vardenafil / avanafil intermediate niche. critical contraindications nitrates — absolute contraindication. severe hypotension. includes sublingual nitroglycerin for chest pain. patients on prn nitroglycerin should not take pde5i within 24 h (sildenafil) or 48 h (tadalafil). α-blockers — relative; can synergize for hypotension. tamsulosin generally safe with pde5i at standard doses, but counsel on orthostasis. recent mi / unstable cardiac status — defer. severe hepatic impairment — reduce dose. side effects headache, flushing, dyspepsia, nasal congestion. vision changes (sildenafil; rare ischemic optic neuropathy reported). priapism — rare; counsel patients to seek care at >4 hours. the teaching point sildenafil and tadalafil are the same mechanism with different pharmacokinetics. pick on the basis of patient preference, dosing pattern (event-driven vs. daily), and other indications (bph-coexistent ed → daily tadalafil is two diagnoses one pill). the danger is always nitrates. ask. twice. s 1·3·5

label: Antibiotics — Urologic Use
text: antibiotics — urologic use · pathogen and tissue penetration drive choice, not "broad-spectrum vs narrow" match site to drug match the antibiotic to the station site / scenario first-line why acute uncomplicated cystitis (station 3) nitrofurantoin 100 mg bid ×5 d or tmp-smx ds bid ×3 d or fosfomycin 3 g ×1 bladder-concentrating agents. nitrofurantoin doesn't reach renal tissue — perfect for cystitis, useless for pyelo. pyelonephritis (station 1) outpatient: ciprofloxacin 500 mg bid ×7 d (if local resistance low). inpatient: ceftriaxone 1 g iv, then tailor. renal tissue penetration required. never use nitrofurantoin or fosfomycin for pyelo — they don't reach parenchymal tissue. acute bacterial prostatitis (station 5) ciprofloxacin or tmp-smx, 2–4 weeks lipid-soluble agents penetrate the inflamed prostate. β-lactams penetrate poorly when uninflamed; in acute prostatitis the barrier is leaky and most agents work — but the duration is what matters. chronic bacterial prostatitis fluoroquinolone 4–6 weeks lipid solubility is the only way through. β-lactams fail. urosepsis with obstruction pip-tazo or carbapenem empirically, plus drainage drainage is the procedure. antibiotics support it; they do not substitute for it. pre-procedure prophylaxis (cystoscopy, trus biopsy) per institutional protocol; rectal swab-guided for prostate biopsy fluoroquinolone resistance has risen sharply; targeted prophylaxis is now standard for trus biopsy. rules that get you 80% of the way nitrofurantoin is a bladder drug. useless for kidney, prostate, blood. useful and clean for uncomplicated cystitis. avoid if crcl <30. fluoroquinolones penetrate everything urologic. including prostate. tendinopathy, dysglycemia, qt prolongation, c. difficile — reserve them. tmp-smx — good cystitis and prostate. watch creatinine bump (tubular secretion, usually not real aki), hyperkalemia, sulfa allergy. ceftriaxone — workhorse inpatient pyelonephritis empirically. easy iv-to-po once cultures back. always send the culture before the first dose when feasible. the culture is what tells you the second week. the asymptomatic bacteriuria rule positive urine culture, no symptoms, no fever, no pyuria-with-pain → do not treat . exceptions: pregnancy, pre-urologic procedure with mucosal trauma expected. treating colonization selects resistance, changes nothing clinically, and turns the next real infection harder to treat. this is the most violated rule in inpatient urinary management. don't be the one who violates it. s 6

label: Phenazopyridine & Desmopressin — Surface Agents
text: phenazopyridine & desmopressin — surface agents · one numbs symptom · one reduces urine production narrow uses phenazopyridine (pyridium) topical urinary analgesic. turns urine and tears bright orange — warn patients, warn the lab. treats nothing, masks dysuria. useful: short-term symptom relief in confirmed uncomplicated cystitis while antibiotics work, 2–3 days max. dangerous: in renal insufficiency (methemoglobinemia, hemolytic anemia), as a substitute for actual diagnosis, or in patients with g6pd deficiency. desmopressin (ddavp) synthetic vasopressin analog. reduces nocturnal urine production. useful: primary nocturnal enuresis in children, nocturnal polyuria in carefully selected adults, mild factor viii deficiency / von willebrand. hyponatremia is the dangerous side effect. restrict fluids around dosing. avoid in older patients with cardiac or renal disease. check baseline sodium; recheck after starting and at follow-up. death from severe hyponatremia is documented and entirely preventable. why these get grouped here both feel innocuous and both cause serious harm when used carelessly. phenazopyridine is a band-aid that hides infection while it climbs. desmopressin is a powerful water-retainer in a population that often cannot tolerate it. treat both as prescribing decisions, not otc suggestions.

label: Two Quick Heuristics
text: two quick heuristics · the "before you sign" checklist which station is the lesion at? which station does this drug act at? is there a pvr i should know before starting it? (mandatory before antimuscarinics in any man, any patient with luts, anyone with neurologic disease.) is the patient on nitrates? (mandatory before any pde5i.) will this drug change a number i care about — psa, creatinine, sodium, bp? document the baseline. the combination logic bph with large prostate and moderate-to-severe symptoms: α-blocker + 5-ari . two mechanisms on the same station — dynamic + structural. oab refractory to monotherapy: antimuscarinic + β3 agonist . two mechanisms on the same station, additive effect. bph + ed: tadalafil 5 mg daily handles both. most of the rational polypharmacy in urology pairs drugs that act at different stations or different mechanisms on the same station — never two drugs of the same class. written may 24, 2026 — for pas, medical students, and clinical clerks ← back to reference

label: The Operations — What Actually Happens
text: the operations — what actually happens · five operations, each acting on a specific station of the conduit. what the surgeon is doing mechanically, what the post-op course should look like, and the complications that come back to you on the call phone. for pas, medical students, and clinical clerks.

label: How To Read This Document
text: how to read this document · every operation has the same shape, and we describe each in that shape. what station does it act on? what does it remove or rearrange? what's the expected day-by-day post-op course? what complications bring the patient back, and which need urology vs. emergency surgery vs. medicine? the point isn't to make you a surgeon. the point is so that when a post-op patient calls or returns and the chart says "pod3 s/p holep" , you have a model in your head of what was just done to that man's prostate, what his urine should look like today, and what would worry the surgeon enough that you should call. the frame, once every urology operation either removes a lesion at a station , reconstructs a conduit segment , or creates a new exit when the existing one cannot work . hold those three categories and the post-op picture for any operation is roughly predictable from first principles.

label: 1 — TURP (Transurethral Resection of the Prostate)
text: 1 — turp (transurethral resection of the prostate) · 5 station

label: TURP
text: turp · station 5 · bph outlet obstruction · endoscopic remove what the operation is removing. the hyperplastic adenoma (yellow) compresses the prostatic urethra and bulges into the bladder neck. resection or enucleation removes that mass and re-opens the station-5 lumen, leaving the true prostatic capsule behind. from burroughs wellcome co., atlas of the urinary tract . what the surgeon actually does a resectoscope passes up the urethra to the prostate. a wire loop carries cutting current and shaves the obstructing adenoma — the inner zone of the prostate — into small chips, which are irrigated out and sent to pathology. the capsule, the verumontanum, and the external sphincter are preserved. hemostasis is the other half of the operation; bleeding from the prostatic fossa is brisk if not controlled. at the end, a 22 fr 3-way foley with cbi sits in the bladder. standard post-op course pod 0 3-way foley with cbi. urine pink to light red, never bright red. patient may have bladder spasms — antimuscarinic prn (oxybutynin or b&o suppository). hgb checked overnight. pod 1 cbi off if urine pink, bpl irrigation as needed. diet advanced. ambulate. most patients go home pod 1 with the catheter still in. pod 2–3 catheter out in clinic or at home with pcp support. patient may have dysuria, urgency, frequency for 2–4 weeks ("raw fossa healing"). coach: drink water, avoid caffeine, expect intermittent pink urine at terminal stream for weeks. weeks 2–6 delayed bleeding is the classic visit — eschar sloughs off the resected fossa around pod 10–21 and patients see frank blood. usually self-limited with hydration; escalate if clot retention or hemodynamic change. complications that come back to you clot retention — most common urgent return. manual evacuation + cbi restart. see procedures doc. delayed hematuria at week 2–3 — eschar sloughing. usually clears with hydration; call urology if it doesn't. uti — common, send culture, treat. tur syndrome (now rare) — historic complication of monopolar turp with hypotonic irrigation, causing hyponatremia and confusion. bipolar turp and holep use saline and have largely eliminated this. still worth knowing the term. retrograde ejaculation — expected, not a complication per se. pre-op counseling. permanent in 65–75%. erectile dysfunction — risk discussed; rates 5–10% above baseline. bladder neck contracture / urethral stricture — late complication, weeks to months. returns with worsening flow. persistent urinary incontinence — should be transient. persistent stress incontinence after 3 months suggests sphincter injury; call. things that should not happen — call now bright red effluent on cbi that doesn't clear with rate increase + manual evacuation. hemodynamic change with hematuria. inability to replace a dislodged foley in the first 48 hours — call before forcing. fever >38.5°c with rigors — urosepsis suspected.

label: 2 — HoLEP (Holmium Laser Enucleation of the Prostate)
text: 2 — holep (holmium laser enucleation of the prostate) · 5 station

label: HoLEP
text: holep · station 5 · bph · endoscopic · laser enucleation + morcellation remove what's different from turp same target — the obstructing adenoma at station 5 — but a different mechanical approach. the holmium laser develops a plane between the adenoma and the surgical capsule (the same plane an open simple prostatectomy uses), the adenoma is enucleated whole into the bladder, and a morcellator inside the bladder breaks it into pieces for removal. the result is more complete removal of adenoma than turp — including very large glands (>100 g) that previously required open surgery. hemostasis is excellent because the laser seals as it cuts. standard post-op course pod 0 3-way foley with cbi, typically lower irrigation rate than turp — less bleeding. often discharge same day in selected centers. pod 1 catheter out, void trial, discharge. faster than turp. first 2 weeks mild dysuria, urge, and possible stress urinary incontinence (transient, mechanism: the external sphincter has just lost its proximal support; trigone control returns within weeks). pelvic floor exercises help. weeks 2–4 symptoms continue improving. flow rates by week 4 are dramatically better than baseline. complications that come back to you early stress urinary incontinence — distinguishing feature from turp. patients leak on cough/exercise for weeks. reassure, refer to pelvic floor pt. most resolve by 3 months. hematuria — less than turp but still present. delayed eschar sloughing at week 2–3 still occurs. uti, urgency, retrograde ejaculation — similar to turp. bladder injury from the morcellator — rare; pre-empted by the surgeon, but if you see hematuria with peritonitis signs early post-op, this is the one to consider. the choice between turp and holep same target. turp is faster to teach, faster to perform on smaller glands, ubiquitously available. holep works on any prostate size, gives more complete tissue removal, and has the bleeding profile to handle anticoagulated patients. for glands >80 g, holep (or simple prostatectomy) is the right operation.

label: 3 — Ureteroscopy (URS) ± Holmium Lithotripsy + Stent
text: 3 — ureteroscopy (urs) ± holmium lithotripsy + stent · 2 station

label: Ureteroscopy
text: ureteroscopy · station 2 · stones, strictures, upper-tract tumors remove + bridge what the surgeon actually does a flexible (or semi-rigid) ureteroscope passes up the urethra, through the bladder, into the ureter through the uvj. the stone is visualized; a holmium laser breaks it into dust or small fragments; a basket retrieves the bigger pieces. a double-j ureteral stent is left in place at the end (in nearly all cases) to ensure drainage through the next 1–14 days while edema resolves. the stent has a loop at the renal pelvis and a loop at the bladder, holding it in. standard post-op course pod 0 same-day discharge in most cases. foley out before discharge. patient takes home a stent. days 1–7 stent symptoms: urgency, frequency, hematuria, suprapubic discomfort, flank pain on voiding (vesicoureteral reflux up the stent). all expected. phenazopyridine prn for dysuria. α-blocker (tamsulosin) sometimes improves stent tolerance. push fluids. days 7–14 stent removal in clinic — simple cystoscopic procedure. symptoms resolve within 24–48 hours of removal. weeks 4–6 repeat imaging (kub or ct) to confirm stone clearance. metabolic stone workup if recurrent. complications that come back to you stent symptoms more severe than expected — first thing: confirm the stent isn't migrated (kub). then anticholinergic + α-blocker + phenazopyridine. early stent removal if intractable. fever post-urs — assume infected obstruction until proven otherwise. ua, culture, cbc, lactate. ct if hydronephrosis suspected (stent should be preventing this — if hydronephrosis is present, the stent is occluded or displaced). forgotten stent — the classic late catastrophe. stents are designed to stay in 2–6 weeks, no longer. encrusted stents that have been in for months become a stone surrounding a stent, removal becomes a major operation, and patients can lose renal function. track every stent you place. ureteral injury / perforation — rare; intraoperative finding usually. post-op presentation: persistent flank pain, urinoma on imaging. hematuria — pink urine for days is expected. frank red blood with clots is not. the stent counseling moment every patient who goes home with a stent gets one sentence drilled into them: "you have a plastic tube in your kidney. if you don't come back to have it taken out, you will lose this kidney." the number of patients lost to follow-up after stent placement is non-trivial. build a tracking system. confirm the removal appointment exists before they leave.

label: 4 — Robotic Radical Prostatectomy (RARP)
text: 4 — robotic radical prostatectomy (rarp) · 5 station

label: Robotic Radical Prostatectomy
text: robotic radical prostatectomy · station 5 · localized prostate cancer · oncologic resection + reconstruction remove + reconstruct why this operation exists. early prostate cancer (left) is contained within the gland — surgically curable. advanced disease (right) invades into bladder and beyond. radical prostatectomy is the curative option for organ-confined disease; locally advanced disease is treated multimodally. from burroughs wellcome co., atlas of the urinary tract . what the surgeon actually does this is a different operation from turp/holep. those leave the prostate behind and just remove the obstructing inner zone. here, the entire prostate gland is removed, plus the seminal vesicles, plus a margin around the apex and base. the bladder neck is reconstructed and anastomosed directly to the urethra over a foley. pelvic lymphadenectomy is performed for staging in many cases. the patient leaves the or with a urethral foley sitting through a fresh anastomosis — that catheter is the operation until the anastomosis heals. standard post-op course pod 0–1 inpatient overnight in most centers; discharge pod 1 typical. foley to gravity, ambulate, diet advanced. drain (if placed) measured for output and creatinine. days 1–7 home with the foley. strict no-instrumentation: any urethral manipulation can disrupt the anastomosis. patient is taught foley care, leg bag use, and when to call. day 7–10 cystogram in clinic if surgeon protocol requires; if no leak, foley out. some surgeons remove without imaging. weeks 1–3 after foley out significant urinary incontinence — this is the expected price. the internal sphincter is gone (it was part of the prostate). continence depends on the external sphincter alone. pelvic floor pt starts immediately. 70–90% achieve continence by 12 months. months 1–3 first psa. should be undetectable (<0.1 ng/ml). rising psa = biochemical recurrence; oncology pathway. months 3–24 erectile function recovery — slower than continence. depends on nerve-sparing technique, age, baseline function. complications that come back to you catheter falls out / pulled in the first 7 days — do not replace blindly. call urology immediately. often requires cystoscopic placement over a wire to avoid disrupting the anastomosis. anastomotic leak — drain output that's urinous, drain creatinine markedly higher than serum. usually heals with prolonged foley drainage and a percutaneous drain. rarely re-operative. lymphocele — fluid collection in the pelvis from disrupted lymphatics. most asymptomatic and resolve. infected lymphocele or compressive lymphocele needs drainage. stress urinary incontinence at 3+ months — expected to varying degrees. persistent sui at 12 months may need surgical management (sling, aus). bladder neck contracture — late complication, narrows the anastomosis. returns with weak stream. erectile dysfunction — expected; managed with pde5i, vacuum devices, intracavernosal injections, eventually penile implant if refractory. rising psa — never normal after rp. >0.2 with confirmation = biochemical recurrence. the post-prostatectomy rule a post-prostatectomy patient with a foley problem in the first 30 days belongs on the operating surgeon's phone. not a generic urology consult. not the ed's catheter cart. the surgeon knows what their anastomosis looks like.

label: 5 — Partial Nephrectomy (Robotic or Open)
text: 5 — partial nephrectomy (robotic or open) · 1 station

label: Partial Nephrectomy
text: partial nephrectomy · station 1 · small renal masses · nephron-sparing remove + reconstruct the architecture being preserved. partial nephrectomy resects a tumor with a margin and reconstructs the cortex and collecting system back together. done well, this preserves nephrons (cortex/medulla mass) and renal function that radical nephrectomy would sacrifice. from burroughs wellcome co., atlas of the urinary tract . what the surgeon actually does the renal hilum is exposed. the renal artery is temporarily clamped (warm ischemia) — the clock starts. the tumor is excised with a small rim of normal parenchyma. the collecting system is closed (if entered). the cortex is reconstructed. the clamp comes off and the kidney reperfuses. the goal: tumor out, collecting system watertight, parenchyma reconstructed, kidney still working. standard post-op course pod 0–1 inpatient. jp drain in the perinephric space. foley overnight. hgb trended. creatinine bumped (expected — single-kidney ischemia time). pod 2–3 foley out. drain output trends: if low and serous, drain comes out before discharge. if high or urinous, drain stays. discharge pod 2–4 typically. weeks 1–4 recovery. restrictions on heavy lifting. watch for delayed bleeding (week 2–3 — pseudoaneurysm rupture territory). month 3, then annually surveillance imaging (ct or mri). renal function monitoring. complications that come back to you urine leak — drain output stays elevated, drain creatinine markedly higher than serum. confirm with drain cr. management: prolonged drain + foley to lower bladder pressure, ± ureteral stent to decompress upstream. most heal in weeks. reoperation rare. delayed bleeding (pseudoaneurysm rupture) — pod 7–21. sudden flank pain, hematuria, hemodynamic change. ct angio, ir embolization. this is a true emergency — recognize the timeline. aki — expected mild rise. significant aki suggests vascular injury, particularly if the patient has a solitary kidney or pre-existing ckd. positive surgical margin — pathology finding. surveillance vs. completion nephrectomy decision is the surgeon's, not ed's. wound infection / hernia at port site — standard surgical complications. why this operation exists instead of radical for small renal masses (<7 cm, t1), partial nephrectomy gives equivalent cancer outcomes to radical nephrectomy and preserves nephrons. preserved nephrons matter long-term — chronic kidney disease, cardiovascular events, and overall survival are all better. radical nephrectomy is reserved for tumors where partial is technically impossible or unsafe.

label: The Quick-Reference Card
text: the quick-reference card · operation station what's removed catheter duration watch for turp 5 · prostate adenoma chips 1–2 days clot retention, week-2 eschar bleed holep 5 · prostate enucleated adenoma (morcellated) 1 day early sui, week-2 eschar bleed ureteroscopy + stent 2 · ureter stone fragments none (stent only) stent intolerance, fever (obstructed infection), forgotten stent rarp 5 · prostate whole prostate + svs 7–14 days dislodged foley (call surgeon), anastomotic leak, sui, rising psa partial nephrectomy 1 · kidney tumor + margin 1–2 days urine leak, week-2–3 pseudoaneurysm bleed

label: The 30-Day Rule
text: the 30-day rule · for any urology post-op patient in the first 30 days any new acute finding — bleeding, fever, can't void, can't pass urine, pain disproportionate to expected course, drain output change — belongs on the operating surgeon's phone , not a generic urology consult and not "let's see in the morning." the surgeon knows their reconstruction. you do not yet. bias toward calling. may 24, 2026 — for pas, medical students, and clinical clerks every operation acts on a station · know which one and you know the post-op picture ← back to reference

label: Clinic Follow-Up Patterns
text: clinic follow-up patterns · the four most common urology clinic returns and how to make each visit useful. psa in context, bladder cancer surveillance, the kidney mass that the radiologist found, and recurrent uti in women and in men. plus the clinic note that makes the next visit possible. for pas, medical students, and clinical clerks.

label: Why Clinic Notes Are Different From Inpatient Notes
text: why clinic notes are different from inpatient notes · on the wards, you write to communicate with the next shift. in clinic, you write to communicate with yourself in six months , who has seen 300 patients between this visit and the next and will not remember a single thing about this one. the clinic note's job is to make the next visit competent without you being there to explain. the clinic-note frame every urology clinic note answers four questions: what conduit station are we monitoring? what did we find or do last time? what's the surveillance interval and what test comes next? what would cause us to escalate? notes that answer those four are useful. notes that don't are decorative.

label: 1 — PSA in Context
text: 1 — psa in context · p

label: Prostate-Specific Antigen
text: prostate-specific antigen · station 5 · screening, surveillance, and a number that means different things in different contexts what psa actually measures a serum glycoprotein produced almost exclusively by prostatic epithelial cells. the total psa reflects the volume of prostatic tissue plus any disruption of the gland's architecture that lets psa leak into the circulation faster than usual. it is not a cancer-specific test. it is a prostate-disruption test. bph raises it. inflammation raises it. recent ejaculation raises it. dre raises it. cancer raises it. how to interpret a single psa context what changes patient on 5α-reductase inhibitor (finasteride, dutasteride) double the psa for interpretation. 6 months of treatment cuts psa ~50%. always document the multiplier in the note. recent acute urinary retention psa artificially elevated for days–weeks. recheck after recovery. recent prostate biopsy or instrumentation recheck in 4–6 weeks; up to 50× elevation is possible acutely. active uti / prostatitis treat the infection first; recheck in 6 weeks. after radical prostatectomy should be undetectable (<0.1 ng/ml). anything detectable is concerning. after radiation therapy nadir varies; failure defined as nadir + 2.0 (phoenix criteria). when psa trend matters more than the single value psa velocity (rate of rise) and psa density (psa / prostate volume) refine risk. a man with a psa of 6 in a 100 g prostate is different from a man with a psa of 6 in a 30 g prostate. the first has a density of 0.06 (low concern), the second 0.20 (significant concern). what to actually do with the number psa elevated, first finding → repeat in 6–12 weeks after addressing reversible causes (uti, retention, recent instrumentation). confirm before acting. persistent elevation → multiparametric prostate mri (replaces or precedes biopsy in most current pathways). pi-rads 3 lesions are equivocal; 4–5 are biopsy targets. mri suspicious, or clinical concern despite normal mri → mri-targeted ± systematic prostate biopsy. psa rising on active surveillance for low-risk cancer → trigger repeat mri ± biopsy. psa rising after definitive treatment → biochemical recurrence; oncology pathway, restaging imaging (psma pet is the modern tool). what the clinic note must contain today's psa, with date. previous psa values (last 3 minimum) with dates — trend is the point. whether the patient is on a 5-ari (and the corrected value if so). prostate volume (dre estimate or imaging) for psa density. any reversible cause to address before next check. the plan: recheck interval, next test, what would trigger biopsy.

label: 2 — Bladder Cancer Surveillance
text: 2 — bladder cancer surveillance · b

label: After TURBT — the cystoscopy schedule
text: after turbt — the cystoscopy schedule · station 3 · non-muscle-invasive bladder cancer is a chronic disease, not a one-time fix the bladder is a busy station. tumors are one of many things that can sit on the wall of station 3. the job of surveillance cystoscopy is to find the new tumor early, while it's still confined to the urothelium and treatable endoscopically. from burroughs wellcome co., atlas of the urinary tract . the framing urothelial cancer is a field-effect disease — the entire urothelium has been exposed to the same insult (typically tobacco) and any one cell can transform. resect a tumor and another may grow in the next year, or the next decade. surveillance is the treatment plan, not an optional follow-up. risk stratification drives the schedule risk group what it looks like low risk solitary, primary, ta low-grade, <3 cm. intermediate risk recurrent, multifocal, or >3 cm ta low-grade. high risk any high-grade, t1, cis, bcg-failure, variant histology. standard surveillance cystoscopy schedule (nmibc) low risk cystoscopy at 3 months. if negative: yearly thereafter for 5 years. discharge if persistently negative. intermediate cystoscopy + cytology every 3 months × 1–2 years, then every 6 months × 2 years, then yearly. upper-tract imaging every 1–2 years. high risk cystoscopy + cytology every 3 months × 2 years, every 6 months × 2 years, yearly thereafter — typically lifelong. upper-tract imaging yearly. consider random biopsies, mmc/bcg maintenance. what the clinic note must contain risk group, stated explicitly. date and findings of the most recent cystoscopy. ("no recurrence" is not enough; describe what was looked at: trigone, lateral walls, dome, neck, scope to verumontanum.) cytology result, dated. last upper-tract imaging (ct urogram or renal us per protocol). intravesical therapy status — bcg induction or maintenance schedule, with cycle numbers. next scope and next imaging dates, explicit. smoking status. always. (smoking cessation reduces recurrence; this is treatment, not lifestyle advice.) the things that escalate new visible tumor on surveillance → re-turbt. positive cytology with negative cystoscopy → look upstream. upper-tract urothelial carcinoma. ct urogram + ureteroscopy. high-grade recurrence after bcg → consider radical cystectomy. conversation, not delay.

label: 3 — The Incidental Kidney Mass
text: 3 — the incidental kidney mass · k

label: Small Renal Mass Workup
text: small renal mass workup · station 1 · 60% of renal masses are found incidentally — and that's the whole reason they're treatable the scenario a man gets a ct for diverticulitis. report mentions a 2.3 cm solid enhancing lesion at the lower pole of the left kidney. he has no urinary symptoms. he has been referred to your clinic. he is terrified. the visit's job is to (a) confirm the finding, (b) characterize the lesion, (c) lay out the options, (d) document the conversation. the characterization questions question why it matters does it enhance with contrast? enhancement (>15–20 hu change) defines a solid mass. non-enhancing = cyst. is it fat-containing? macroscopic fat on ct (<–10 hu) is angiomyolipoma — benign. don't operate. is it cystic? bosniak category? bosniak i–ii: benign, observe. iif: surveillance. iii–iv: surgical, malignancy risk significant. how big? <4 cm = t1a, partial nephrectomy or active surveillance. 4–7 cm = t1b, partial if feasible. >7 cm = t2, often radical. where is it? polar lesions are easier to resect than central or hilar lesions. exophytic vs. endophytic. nephrometry score quantifies this. the three branches of the algorithm partial nephrectomy — small enhancing solid mass, otherwise healthy patient. curative if confined. active surveillance — small (<3 cm), older patient, significant comorbidity, slow growth on serial imaging. many small renal masses are indolent. repeat imaging at 3, 6, 12 months then yearly. ablation (cryoablation, rfa) — small, peripheral, poor surgical candidate. lower-quality oncologic data than partial nephrectomy but reasonable for selected patients. biopsy when? renal mass biopsy used to be reserved for unusual cases (suspected metastatic deposit, lymphoma, abscess). modern practice biopsies more often — especially before ablation, before observing a mass, or when imaging is indeterminate. a benign biopsy result (oncocytoma, aml) can avoid an unnecessary nephrectomy. bleeding and seeding risks are real but small. what the clinic note must contain mass description: size, location, density, enhancement, bosniak (if cystic), growth rate (if serial imaging). renal function (egfr), single vs. two kidneys. patient comorbidities and surgical fitness. options discussed (surgery / surveillance / ablation), shared decision-making documented. next imaging or treatment date, explicit.

label: 4 — Recurrent UTI
text: 4 — recurrent uti · u

label: Recurrent Urinary Tract Infection
text: recurrent urinary tract infection · station 3 (mostly) · in women, look for behavior and biology · in men, look for anatomy recurrent uti = ≥2 episodes in 6 months, or ≥3 in 12 months. the clinic visit splits into two completely different conversations depending on the patient's sex.

label: Recurrent UTI in women
text: recurrent uti in women · in a structurally normal female urinary tract, recurrence is the rule rather than the exception. the mechanism is colonization of the vaginal vestibule and periurethral area by uropathogens that migrate up a short urethra. anatomy is rarely the problem; behavior, biology, and bacterial reservoirs are. workup urine culture every episode for one cycle of recurrences — confirm it's actually uti, identify the organism, check resistance pattern. post-void residual in office — rule out retention as a substrate. imaging only if atypical: stone history, hematuria, atypical organism (proteus suggests stone), pyelonephritis episodes. cystoscopy for atypical features or persistent unexplained recurrence. management modifiable behaviors — voiding after intercourse, hydration, wiping direction, avoiding spermicide. evidence is mixed; counseling is cheap. vaginal estrogen in postmenopausal women — single most effective intervention. restores vaginal ph and lactobacillus dominance. topical, low systemic absorption, safe in most. cranberry, d-mannose, probiotics — modest or unclear evidence; reasonable to try. antibiotic strategies — patient-initiated short course (give the prescription, let her start at first symptoms after sending culture); post-coital prophylaxis; daily low-dose prophylaxis (last resort — resistance and microbiome cost).

label: Recurrent UTI in men
text: recurrent uti in men · why a man with recurrent uti gets imaged. every label in this figure is a potential reservoir for infection. stagnant urine in any of these (a diverticulum, an obstructed bladder, a stone, a stricture) is where bacteria persist between courses of antibiotics. from burroughs wellcome co., atlas of the urinary tract . a man with recurrent uti has anatomy. period. the long male urethra and the prostatic barrier make uti uncommon, so when it recurs, find the enabler . that is the entire workup. workup urine culture — confirm, identify, treat the episode. post-void residual — retention is a common substrate (bph). renal/bladder ultrasound or ct urogram — stones, hydronephrosis, bladder diverticulum, masses. cystoscopy — diverticula, bladder stones, foreign bodies (forgotten stent), bladder cancer, strictures. dre and psa — chronic prostatitis vs. cancer in the differential. expressed prostatic secretions / meares-stamey four-glass test in selected cases of suspected chronic bacterial prostatitis. management treat the enabling lesion. stone → remove. bph with retention → manage outlet. diverticulum with stagnant urine → consider resection. the infection is the symptom; the anatomy is the disease. chronic bacterial prostatitis — needs prolonged courses (4–6 weeks) of lipid-soluble antibiotics that penetrate prostatic tissue (fluoroquinolone, tmp-smx). cure rate is honest with patients: variable. prophylactic antibiotics are a last resort and rarely curative in men because they don't fix anatomy. the asymmetry a 60-year-old woman with three utis a year is common and usually has none of the dangerous anatomy. a 60-year-old man with three utis a year almost certainly has something obstructing or harboring bacteria. different sex, same diagnosis, completely different workup.

label: The Clinic Note Template
text: the clinic note template · one template, useful for almost every urology visit. the headers are stations of the conduit plus surveillance variables. the note's purpose is to make the next visit possible without reconstruction. subjective reason for visit. luts by ipss if relevant. pain, hematuria, incontinence (stress/urge/mixed), voiding symptoms. sexual function (erectile, ejaculatory). prior treatments, response. pertinent negatives. objective vitals. focused exam: abdomen (palpable bladder?), back (cva tenderness), gu (meatus, prepuce, scrotum, testes, cord), dre (size, consistency, nodules, tenderness). data latest ua, culture (date, organism). psa trend (last 3 with dates; on 5-ari? doubled?). imaging: ct/us/mri/cystogram — date, key findings. procedures: cysto/turbt/biopsy — date, findings. pvr if measured today. egfr / creatinine trend if relevant. assessment — by station station 1 (kidney): … station 2 (ureter): … station 3 (bladder): … station 4 (sphincter): … station 5 (outlet): … active surveillance protocols and their cycle numbers. plan - today's interventions (med changes, in-office procedures). - next test, with date. - next visit, with timing. - what would escalate before next visit (named symptoms, threshold values). - patient education delivered. why the station-by-station assessment matters a man being followed for low-risk prostate cancer (station 5) may also have chronic kidney disease (station 1), a known bladder diverticulum (station 3), and stress urinary incontinence (station 4). the single-diagnosis note misses three problems. the station-by-station note is auditable — anyone reading it knows what's being watched at each level of the conduit and what comes next.

label: One More Discipline — The Surveillance Calendar
text: one more discipline — the surveillance calendar · urology generates more surveillance schedules per patient than most specialties. the patient cannot remember them. the chart often won't enforce them. build a visible calendar inside every chart. for every patient under surveillance, on the front page of the chart diagnosis being surveilled, with date of diagnosis. risk category (low / intermediate / high) where applicable. scheduled test #1, date, status. scheduled test #2, date, status. trigger criteria for unscheduled visits. end-of-surveillance condition (e.g. "after 5 years cystoscopy-negative, transition to pcp-led monitoring"). this is not paperwork. it is the part of the disease management plan that survives between visits — and the part that prevents the forgotten stent, the missed surveillance cystoscopy that lets a high-grade recurrence become muscle-invasive, the post-prostatectomy patient whose psa started rising in month 18 and nobody saw because nobody scheduled the draw. may 24, 2026 — for pas, medical students, and clinical clerks the clinic note's job is to make the next visit possible · station by station · with the calendar attached ← back to reference
